parturient: Bringing forth or about to bring forth young.
parturition: The act of bringing forth or being delivered of young.
party: Anumber of persons united in opinion or design, in opposition to others in the community. It differs from faction, in implying a less dishonorable association, or more justifiable designs. Parties exist in all governments; and free governments are the hot-beds of party. Formerly, the political parties in England were called whigs and tories.
One of two litigants; the plaintiff or defendant in a lawsuit. The cause of both parties shall come before the judges. Exodus 22:9.
One concerned or interested in an affair. This man was not a party to the trespass or affray. He is not a party to the contract or agreement.
Side; persons engaged against each other. The peace both parties want, is like to last. Small parties make up in diligence what they want in numbers.
Cause; side. Aegle came in to make their party good.
party-colored: Having divers colors; as a party-colored plume; a party-colored flower.
party-jury: A jury consisting of half natives and half foreigners.
party-man: One of a party; usually, a factious man; a man of violent party principles; an abettor of a party.
party-spirit: The spirit that supports a party.
party-wall: A wall that separates one house from the next.
pasch: The passover; the feast of Easter.
paschal: Pertaining to the passover, or to Easter.
pasch-egg: An egg stained and presented to young persons, about the time of Easter.
pashaw: In the Turkish dominions, a viceroy, governor or commander; a bashaw.
pasigraphy: A system of universal writing, or a manner of writing that may be understood and used by all nations.
pasque-flower: pask’-flower. A flower, a species of anemone.
pasquil: A mutilated statue at Rome, in a corner of the palace of Ursini, so called from a cobbler of that name who was remarkable for his sneers and gibes. On this statue it has been customary to paste satiric papers. Hence, a lampoon.
pasquin: A mutilated statue at Rome, in a corner of the palace of Ursini, so called from a cobbler of that name who was remarkable for his sneers and gibes. On this statue it has been customary to paste satiric papers. Hence, a lampoon.
pass: To move, in almost any manner; to go; to proceed from one place to another. A man may pass on foot, on horseback or in a carriage; a bird and a meteor pass through the air; a ship passes on or through the water; light passes from the sun to the planets; it passes from the sun to the earth in about eight minutes.
To move from one state to another; to alter or change, or to be changed in condition; as, to pass from health to sickness; to pass from just to unjust.
To vanish; to disappear; to be lost. In this sense, we usually say, to pass away. Beauty is a charm, but soon the charm will pass.
To be spent; to go on or away progressively. The time when the thing existed, is the idea of that space of duration which passed between some fixed period and the being of that thing.
To die; to depart from life.
pass-parole: In military affairs, a command given at the head of an army and communicated by word of mouth to the rear.
passable: That may be passed, traveled or navigated. The roads are not passable. The stream is passable in boats.
That may be penetrated; as a substance passable by a fluid.
Current; receivable; that may be or is transferred from hand to hand; as bills passable in lieu of coin. False coin is not passable.
passade: In the menage, a turn or course of a horse backwards or forwards on the same spot of ground.
passage: The act of passing or moving by land or water, or through the air or other substance; as the passage of a man or a carriage; the passage of a ship or a fowl; the passage of light or a meteor; the passage of fluids through the pores of the body, or from the glands. Clouds intercept the passage of solar rays.
The time of passing from one place to another. What passage had you? We had a passage of twenty five days to Havre de Grace, and of thirty eight days from England.
Road; way; avenue; a place where men or things may pass or be conveyed. And with his pointed dart, Explores the nearest passage to this heart.
Entrance or exit. What! are my doors opposed against my passage?
Right of passing; as, to engage a passage on board a ship bound to India.
passager: A traveler or voyager; one who passes or journeys on foot, in a vehicle, or in a ship or boat. This word is usually written corruptly passenger, and the first vowel is often short.
passed: Enacted; having received all the formalities necessary to constitute a law.
past: Enacted; having received all the formalities necessary to constitute a law.
of pass. Gone by or beyond; not present; not future.
passenger: One who is traveling, as in a public coach, or in a ship, or on foot. This is the usual, though corrupt orthography. Passenger falcon, a kind of migratory hawk.
passerine: Pertaining to sparrows, or to the order of birds to which sparrows belong, the Passeres.
passibility: The quality or capacity of receiving impressions from external agents; aptness to feel or suffer.
passible: Susceptible of feeling or of impressions from external agents. Apollinarius held even Deity to be passible.
passing: Adverbially used to enforce or enhance the meaning of another word; exceedingly; as passing fair; passing strange.
passing-bell: The bell that rings at the hour of death to obtain prayers for the passing soul. It is also used for the bell that rings immediately after death.
passing-note: In music, a note introduced between two others for the purpose of softening a distance or melodizing a passage.
passion: The impression or effect of an external agent upon a body; that which is suffered or received. A body at rest affords us no idea of any active power to move, and when set in motion, it is rather a passion than an action in it.
Susceptibility of impressions from external agents. The differences of moldable and not moldable, and many other passions of matter, are plebeian notions.
Suffering; emphatically, the last suffering of the Savior. To whom also he showed himself alive after his passion, by many infallible proofs. Acts 1:3.

The feeling of the mind, or the sensible effect of impression; excitement, perturbation or agitation of mind; as desire, fear, hope, joy, grief, loveThe eloquence of the orator is employed to move the passions.
Violent agitation or excitement of mind, particularly such as is occasioned by an offense, injury or insult; hence, violent anger.
passion-flower: A flower and plant of the genus Passiflora.
passion-week: The week immediately preceding the festival of Easter; so called because in that week our Savior’s passion and death took place.
passionary: A book in which are described the sufferings of saints and martyrs.
passionate: Easily moved to anger; easily excited or agitated by injury or insult; applied to persons. Homer’s Achilles is haughty and passionate.
Highly excited; vehement; warm; applied to things; as passionate affection; passionate desire; passionate concern.
Expressing strong emotion; animated; as passionate eloquence.
To affect with passion; to express passionately.
passionately: With passion; with strong feeling; ardently; vehemently; as, to covet any thing passionately; to be passionately fond.
Angrily; with vehement resentment; as, to speak passionately.
passionateness: State of being subject to passion or anger.
passionless: Not easily excited to anger; of a calm temper.
passive: Suffering; not acting, receiving or capable of receiving impressions from external agents. We were passive spectators, not actors in the scene. The mind is wholly passive in the reception of all its simple ideas. God is not in any respect passive.

Unresisting; not opposing; receiving or suffering without resistance; as passive obedience; passive submission to the laws. Passive verb, in grammar, is a verb which expresses passion, or the effect of an action of some agent; as in L. doceor, I am taught; in English, she is loved and admired by her friends; he is assailed by slander. Passive obedience, as used by writers on government, denotes not only quiet unresting submission to power, but implies the denial of the right of resistance, or the recognition of the duty to submit in all cases to the existing government. Passive prayer, among mystic divines, is suspension of the activity of the soul or intellectual faculties, the soul remaining quiet and yielding only to the impulses of grace. Passive commerce, trade in which the productions of a country are carried by foreigners in their own bottoms. [See Active commerce.I .
passively: With a passive nature or temper; with a temper disposed to submit to the acts of external agents, without resistance.
According to the form of the passive verb.
passiveness: Quality of receiving impressions from external agents or causes; as the passiveness of matter.
Passibility; capacity of suffering. We shall lose our passiveness with our being.
passivity: The tendency of a body to persevere in a given state, either of motion or rest, till disturbed by another body.
passover: A feast of the Jews, instituted to commemorate the providential escape of the Hebrews, in Egypt, when God smiting the first-born of the Egyptians, passed over the houses of the Israelites, which were marked with the blood of the paschal lamb.
The sacrifice offered at the feast of the passover.
passport: A written license from a king or other proper authority, granting permission or safe conduct for one to pass through his territories, or to pass from one country to another, or to navigate a particular sea without hindrance or molestation.
A license for importing or exporting contraband goods or movables without paying the usual duties.
That which enables one to pass with safety or certainty. His passport is his innocence and grace.
passy-measure: An old stately kind of dance; a cinque-pace.
paste: A soft composition of substances, as flour moistened with water or milk and kneaded, or any kind of earth moistened and formed to the consistence of dough. Paste made of flour is used in cookery; paste made of flour or earth, is used in various arts and manufactures, as a cement.
An artificial mixture in imitation of precious stones or gems, used in the glass trade.
In mineralogy, the mineral substance in which other minerals are imbedded.
To unite or cement with paste; to fasten with paste.
pasteboard: A species of thick paper formed of several single sheets pasted one upon another, or by macerating paper and casting it in moldsIt is used for the covering of books, for bonnets
pastel: A plant, the woad, of the genus Isatis.
pastern: The part of a horse’s leg between the joint next the foot and the coronet of the hoof.
pastern-joint: The joint in a horse’s leg next the foot.
pastil: A roll of paste, or a kind of paste made of different colors ground with gum-water in order to make crayons.
In pharmacy, a dry composition of sweet smelling resins, aromatic woodsburnt to clear and scent the air of a room.
pastime: Sport; amusement; diversion; that which amuses and serves to make time pass agreeably.
pastor: A shepherd; one that has the care of flocks and herds.
A minister of the gospel who has the charge of a church and congregation, whose duty is to watch over the people of his charge, and instruct them in the sacred doctrines of the christian religion.
pastoral: Pertaining to shepherds; as a pastoral life; pastoral manners.
Descriptive of the life of shepherds; as a pastoral poem.
Relating to the care of souls, or to the pastor of a church; as pastoral care or duties; a pastoral letter. Piety is the life and soul of pastoral fidelity: .

A poem describing the life and manners of shepherds, or a poem in imitation of the action of a shepherd, and in which the speakers take upon themselves the character of shepherds; an idyl; a bucolic. A pastoral is a poem in which any action or passion is represented by its effects on a country life.
pastorate: The office, state or jurisdiction of a spiritual pastor.
pastorship: The office or rank of pastor.
pastry: Things in general which are made of paste, or of which paste constitutes a principal ingredient, as pies, tarts, cake and the like.
The place where pastry is made.
pastry-cook: One whose occupation is to make and sell articles made of paste.
pasturage: The business of feeding or grazing cattle.
Grazing ground; land appropriated to grazing.
pasture: Grass for the food of cattle; the food of cattle taken by grazing.
Ground covered with grass appropriated for the food or cattle. The farmer has a hundred acres of pasture. It is sometimes called pasture-land.
Human culture; education. Common of pasture, is the right of feeding cattle on another’s ground.
To feed on grass or to supply grass for food. We apply the word to persons, as the farmer pastures fifty oxen; or to ground, as the land will pasture fifty oxen.
To graze; to take food by eating grass from the ground.
pasty: Like paste; of the consistence of paste.
A pie made of paste and baked without a dish.
pat: Fit; convenient; exactly suitable either as to time or place.
A light quick blow or stroke with the fingers or hand.
To strike gently with the fingers or hand; to tap. Gay pats my shoulder and you vanish quite.
pataca: A Spanish coin of the value of 4s. 8d. sterling, or about $1. cents.
patacoon: A Spanish coin of the value of 4s. 8d. sterling, or about $1. cents.
patache: A tender or small vessel employed in conveying men or orders from one ship or place to another.
patavinity: The use of local words, or the peculiar style or diction of Livy, the Roman historian; so denominated from Patavium or Padua, the place of his nativity.
patch: A piece of cloth sewed on a garment to repair it.
A small piece of any thing used to repair a breach.
A small piece of silk used to cover a defect on the face, or to add a charm.
A piece inserted in mosaic or variegated work.
A small piece of ground, or a small detached piece.
patched: Mended with a patch or patches; mended clumsily.
patching: Mending with a piece or pieces; botching.
patchwork: Work composed of pieces of various figures sewed together.
Work composed of pieces clumsily put together.
pate: The head, or rather the top of the head; applied to persons, it is now used in contempt or ridicule.
The skin of a calfs head.
In fortification, a kind of platform resembling what is called a horse shoe.
pated: In composition, having a pate; as long-pated, cunning; shallow-pated, having weak intellect.
patee: In heraldry, a cross small in the center, and widening to the extremities which are broad.
pattee: In heraldry, a cross small in the center, and widening to the extremities which are broad.
patefaction: The act of opening or manifesting; open declaration.
patelliform: Of the form of a dish or saucer.
patellite: Fossil remains of the patella, a shell.
paten: In the Romish church, the cover of the chalice, used for holding particles of the host.
patin: In the Romish church, the cover of the chalice, used for holding particles of the host.
patent: In botany, spreading; forming an acute angle with the stem or branch; as a patent leaf.
Open to the perusal of all; as letters patent.
Appropriated by letters patent. Madder--in the time of Charles the first, was made a patent commodity.

A writing given by the proper authority and duly authenticated, granting a privilege to some person or persons. By patent, or letters patent, that is, open letters, the king of Great Britain grants lands, honors and franchises.
patented: Granted by patent; secured by patent or by law as an exclusive privilege.
patentee: One to whom a grant is made or a privilege secured by patent or by law.
patenting: Granting by patent; securing as a privilege.
patent-rolls: The records or registers of patents.
paternal: Pertaining to a father; fatherly; as paternal care of affection; paternal favor or admonition.
Derived from the father; hereditary; as a paternal estate.
paternity: Fathership; the relation of a father. The world, while it had scarcity of people, underwent no other dominion than paternity and eldership.
path: A way beaten or trodden by the feet of man or beast, or made hard by wheels; that part of a highway on which animals or carriages ordinarily pass; applied to the ground only, and never to a paved street in a city: .
Any narrow way beaten by the foot.
The way, course or track where a body moves in the atmosphere or in space; as the path of a planet or comet; the path of a meteor.
Course of life. He marketh all my paths. Job 33:17.
pathetic: Affecting or moving the passions, particularly pity, sorrow, grief or other tender emotion; as a pathetic song or discourse; pathetic expostulation. No theory of the passions can teach a man to be pathetic.

Style or manner adapted to awaken the passions, especially tender emotions. A musician at Venice is said to have so excelled in the pathetic, as to be able to play any of his auditors into distraction.
pathetical: Affecting or moving the passions, particularly pity, sorrow, grief or other tender emotion; as a pathetic song or discourse; pathetic expostulation. No theory of the passions can teach a man to be pathetic.
pathetically: In such a manner as to excite the tender passions.
patheticalness: The quality of moving the tender passions.
pathic: A catamite; a male that submits to the crime against nature.
pathless: Having no beaten way; untrodden; as a pathless forest; a pathless coast.
pathognomonic: Indicating that which is inseparable from a disease, being found in that and in no other; hence, indicating that by which a disease may be certainly known; characteristic; as pathognomonic symptoms.
pathognomy: Expression of the passions; the science of the signs by which human passions are indicated.
pathology: That part of medicine which explains the nature of diseases, their causes and symptoms; or the doctrine of the causes and nature of diseases, comprehending nosology, etiology, symptomatology, and therapeutics.
pathos: Passion; warmth or vehemence, in a speaker; or in language, that which excites emotions and passions.
pathway: A path; usually, a narrow way to be passed on foot.
A way; a course of life. Proverbs 12:28.
patible: Sufferable; tolerable; that may be endured.
patibulary: Belonging to the gallows, or to execution on the cross.
patience: The suffering of afflictions, pain, toil, calamity, provocation or other evil, with a calm, unruffled temper; endurance without murmuring or fretfulness. Patience may spring from constitutional fortitude, from a kind of heroic pride, or from christian submission to the divine will.
A calm temper which bears evils without murmuring or discontent.
The act or quality of waiting long for justice or expected good without discontent. Have patience with me, and I will pay thee all. Matthew 18:26.
Perseverance; constancy in labor or exertion. He learnt with patience, and with meekness taught.
patient: Having the quality of enduring evils without murmuring or fretfulness; sustaining afflictions of body or mind with fortitude, calmness or christian submission to the divine will; as a patient person, or a person of patient temper. It is followed by of before the evil endured; as patient of labor or pain; patient of heat or cold.
Not easily provoked; calm under the sufferance of injuries or offenses; not revengeful. Be patient towards all men.7 Thessalonians 5:14.
Persevering; constant in pursuit or exertion; calmly diligent. Whatever I have done is due to patient thought.
Not hasty; not over eager or impetuous; waiting or expecting with calmness or without discontent. Not patient to expect the turns of fate.
patiently: With calmness or composure; without discontent or murmuring. Submit patiently to the unavoidable evils of life.
With calm and constant diligence; as, to examine a subject patiently.
Without agitation, uneasiness or discontent; without undue haste or eagerness; as, to wait patiently for more favorable events.
patriarch: The father and ruler of a family; one who governs by paternal right. It is usually applied to the progenitors of the Israelites, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob and the sons of Jacob, or to the heads of families before the flood; as the antediluvian patriarchs.
A learned and distinguished character among the Jews.
In the christian church, a dignitary superior to the order of archbishops; as the patriarch of Constantinople, of Alexandria, or of Ephesus.
patriarchal: Belonging to patriarchs; possessed by patriarchs; as patriarchal power or jurisdiction; a patriarchal see.
Subject to a patriarch; as a patriarchal church. Patriarchal cross, in heraldry, is that where the shaft is twice crossed, the lower arms being longer than the upper ones.
patriarchic: Belonging to patriarchs; possessed by patriarchs; as patriarchal power or jurisdiction; a patriarchal see.
Subject to a patriarch; as a patriarchal church. Patriarchal cross, in heraldry, is that where the shaft is twice crossed, the lower arms being longer than the upper ones.
patriarchate: The office, dignity or jurisdiction of a patriarch or ecclesiastical superior.
patriarchship: The office, dignity or jurisdiction of a patriarch or ecclesiastical superior.
patriarchy: The jurisdiction of a patriarch; a patriarchate.
patrician: Senatorial; noble; not plebeian. This epithet is derived from the Roman patres, fathers, the title of Roman senators; as patrician birth or blood; patrician families.
A nobleman. In the Roman state, the patricians were the descendants of the first Roman senators.
patrimonial: Pertaining to a patrimony; inherited from ancestors; as a patrimonial estate.
patrimony: A right or estate inherited from one’s ancestors.
A church estate or revenue; as St. Peter’s patrimony.
patriot: A person who loves his country, and zealously supports and defends it and its interests. Such tears as patriots shed for dying laws.
Patriotic; devoted to the welfare of one’s country; as patriot zeal.
patriotic: Full of patriotism; actuated by the love of one’s country; as a patriotic hero or statesman.
Inspired by the love of one’s country; directed to the public safety and welfare; as patriotic zeal.
patriotism: n. Love of one’s country; the passion which aims to serve one’s country, either in defending it from invasion, or protecting its rights and maintaining its laws and institutions in vigor and purity. Patriotism is the characteristic of a good citizen, the noblest passion that animates a man in the character of a citizen.
patristic: Pertaining to the ancient fathers of the christian church.
patristical: Pertaining to the ancient fathers of the christian church.
patrol: In war, a round; a walking or marching round by a guard in the night, to watch and observe what passes, and to secure the peace and safety of a camp or other place.
The guard or persons who go the rounds for observation; a detachment whose duty is to patroll. In France, there is an army of patrols to secure her fiscal regulations.
patroll: In war, a round; a walking or marching round by a guard in the night, to watch and observe what passes, and to secure the peace and safety of a camp or other place.
The guard or persons who go the rounds for observation; a detachment whose duty is to patroll. In France, there is an army of patrols to secure her fiscal regulations.
To go the rounds in a camp or garrison; to march about and observe what passes; as a guard.
patrolling: Going the rounds, as a guard.
patron: Among the Romans, a master who had freed his slave, and retained some rights over him after his emancipation; also, a man of distinction under whose protection another placed himself. Hence,
One who countenances, supports and protects either a person or a work.
In the church of Rome, a guardian or saint, whose name a person bears, or under whose special care he is placed and whom he invokes; or a saint in whose name a church or order is founded.
In the canon or common law, one who has the gift and disposition of a benefice.
An advocate; a defender; one that specially countenances and supports, or lends aid to advance; as patrons of the arts; a patron of useful undertakings; the patrons of virtue.
patronage: Special countenance or support; favor or aid afforded to second the views of a person or to promote a design.
Advowson; the right of presentation to a church or ecclesiastical benefice.
patronal: Doing the office of a patron; protecting; supporting; favoring; defending.
patroness: A female that favors, countenances or supports. Now night came down, and rose full soon . That patroness of rogues, the moon.
A female that has the right of presenting to a church living.
patronize: To support; to countenance; to defend; as a patron his client.
To favor; to lend aid to promote; as an undertaking.
To maintain; to defend; to support. This idea has been patronized by two states only.
patronizer: One that supports, countenances or favors.
patronymic: A name of men or women derived from that of their parents or ancestors; as Tydides, the son of Tydeus, Pelides, the son of Peleus, that is, Achilles.
patten: The base of a column or pillar.
A wooden shoe with an iron ring, worn to keep the shoes from the dirt or mud.
patter: To strike, as falling drops of water or hail, with a quick succession of small sounds; as pattering hail. The stealing shower is scarce to patter heard.
pattering: Striking with a quick succession of small sounds.
pattern: An original or model proposed for imitation; the archetype; an exemplar; that which is to be copies or imitated, either in things or in actions; as the pattern of a machine; a pattern of patience. Christ was the most perfect pattern of rectitude, patience and submission ever exhibited on earth.
A specimen; a sample; a part showing the figure or quality of the whole; as a pattern of silk cloth.
Any thing cut of formed into the shape of something to be made after it. To pattern after, to imitate; to follow.
patty-pan: A pan to bake a little pie in.
patulous: Spreading, as a patulous calyx; bearing the flowers loose or dispersed, as a patulous peduncle.
pauciloquy: The utterance of few words. J .
paucity: Fewness; smallness of number; as the paucity of schools.
Smallness of quantity; as paucity of blood.
paum: To impose by fraud; a corruption of palm.
paunch: The belly and its contents. The paunch, in ruminating quadrupeds, is the first and largest stomach, into which the food is received before rumination.
To pierce or rip the belly; to eviscerate; to take out the contents of the belly.
pauper: A poor person; particularly, one so indigent as to depend on the parish or town for maintenance.
pauperism: The state of being poor or destitute of the means of support; the state of indigent persons requiring support from the community. The increase of pauperism is an alarming evil.
pause: A stop; a cessation or intermission of action, of speaking, singing, playing or the like; a temporary stop or rest.
Cessation proceeding from doubt; suspense. I stand in pause where I shall first begin.

A temporary cessation in reading. The use of punctuation is to mark the pauses in writing. In verse, there are two kinds of pauses, the cesural and the final. The cesural pause divides the verse; the final pause closes it. The pauses which mark the sense, and which may be called sentential, are the same in prose and verse.
A mark of cessation or intermission of the voice; a point.
pauser: s as z. One who pauses; one who deliberates.
pausing: Stopping for a time; ceasing to speak or act; deliberating.
pavan: A grave dance among the Spaniards. In this dance, the performers make a kind of wheel before each other, the gentlemen dancing with cap and sword, princes with long robes, and the ladies with long trails; the motions resembling the stately steps of the peacock.
pave: To lay or cover with stone or brick so as to make a level or convenient surface for horses, carriages or foot passengers; to floor with brick or stone; as, to pave a street; to pave a side-walk; to pave a court or stable.
To prepare a passage; to facilitate the introduction of. The invention of printing paved the way for intellectual improvement.
paved: Laid over with stones or bricks; prepared; as a way.
pavement: A floor or covering consisting of stones or bricks, laid on the earth in such a manner as to make a hard and convenient passage; as a pavement of pebbles, of bricks, or of marble.
To pave; to floor with stone or brick.
paver: One who lays stones for a floor, or whose occupation is to pave.
pavier: One who lays stones for a floor, or whose occupation is to pave.
pavilion: A tent; a temporary movable habitation.

In architecture, a kind of turret or building, usually insulated and contained under a single roof; sometimes square and sometimes in the form of a dome. Sometimes a pavilion is a projecting part in the front of a building; sometimes it flanks a corner.
In military affairs, a tent raised on posts. The word is sometimes used for a flag, colors, ensign or banner.
In heraldry, a covering in form of a tent, investing the armories of kings.
pavilioned: Furnished with pavilions; sheltered by a tent.
paving: Pavement; a floor of stones or bricks.
pavo: A constellation in the southern hemisphere, consisting of fourteen stars; also a fish.
pavonine: Resembling the tail of a peacock; iridescent.
paw: The foot of beasts of prey having claws, as the lion, the tiger, the dog, catLeviticus 11:27.
To draw the fore foot along the ground; to scrape with the fore foot; as a fiery horse, pawing with his hoof. He paweth in the valley. Job 39:21.
To scrape with the fore foot. His hot courser paw’d th’ Hungarian plain.
pawl: Among seamen, a short bar of wood or iron fixed close to the capstan or windlass of a ship to prevent it from rolling back or giving way.
pawn: Something given or deposited as security for the payment of money borrowed; a pledge. Pawn is applied only to goods, chattels or money, and not to real estate. Men will not take pawns without use.
A pledge for the fulfillment of a promise.
Acommon man at chess. In pawn, at pawn, the state of being pledged. PAWIN. Sweet wife, my honor is at pawn.
To give or deposit in pledge, or as security for the payment of money borrowed; to pledge; as, she pawned the last piece of plate.
To pledge for the fulfillment of a promise; as, to pawn one’s word or honor that an agreement shall be fulfilled.
pawn-broker: One who lends money on pledge or the deposit of goods.
pawnee: The person to whom a pawn is delivered as security; one that takes any thing in pawn. If the pawn is laid up and the pawnee robbed, he is not answerable.
pawner: One that pledges any thing as security for the payment of borrowed money.
pawning: Pledging, as goods; giving as security.
pax: A little image or piece of board with the image of Christ upon the cross on it, which people before the reformation, used to kiss after the service; the ceremony being considered as the kiss of peace.
pay: To discharge a debt; to deliver to a creditor the value of the debt, either in money or goods, to his acceptance or satisfaction, by which the obligation of the debtor is discharged.
To discharge a duty created by promise or by custom or by the moral law; as, to pay a debt of honor or of kindness. You have paid down . More penitence, than done trespass.
To fulfill; to perform what is promised; as, to pay one’s vows.
To render what is due to a superior, or demanded by civility or courtesy; as, to pay respect to a magistrate; to pay due honor to parents.
payable: That may or ought to be paid. In general, money is payable as soon as it is due, or at the time payment is stipulated, or at the expiration of the credit; but by the usage of merchants, three or more days of grace are allowed to the debtor, and a note due at the day when payment is promised, is not payable till the expiration of the days of grace.
That can be paid; that there is power to pay. Thanks are a tribute payable by the poorest.
pay-bill: A bill of money to be paid to the soldiers of a company.
pay-day: The day when payment is to be made or debts discharged; the day on which wages or money is stipulated to be paid.
payee: The person to whom money is to be paid; the person named in a bill or note to whom the amount is promised or directed to be paid.
payer: One that pays. In bills of exchange, the person on whom the bill is drawn, and who is directed to pay the money to the holder.
paymaster: One who is to pay; one from whom wages or reward is received.
In the army, an officer whose duty is to pay the officers and soldiers their wages, and who is entrusted with money for this purpose.
payment: The act of paying, or giving compensation.
The thing given in discharge of a debt or fulfillment of a promise.
pay-office: A place or office where payment is made of public debts.
pea: A plant and its fruit of the genus Pisum, of many varieties. This plant has a papilionaceous flower, and the pericarp is a legume, called in popular language a pod. In the plural, we write peas, for two or more individual seeds, but pease, for an indefinite number in quantity of bulk. We write two, three or four peas, but a bushel of pease.
peace: In a general sense, a state of quiet or tranquillity; freedom from disturbance or agitation; applicable to society, to individuals, or to the temper of the mind.
Freedom from war with a foreign nation; public quiet.
Freedom from internal commotion or civil war.
Freedom from private quarrels, suits or disturbance.

Freedom from agitation or disturbance by the passions, as from fear, terror, anger, anxiety or the like; quietness of mind; tranquillity; calmness; quiet of conscience. Great peace have they that love the law. Psalm 119:165.
peaceable: Free from war, tumult or public commotion. We live in peaceable times. The reformation was introduced in a peaceable manner.
Free from private feuds or quarrels. The neighbors are peaceable. These men are peaceable.
Quiet; undisturbed; not agitated with passion. His mind is very peaceable.
Not violent, bloody or unnatural; as, to die a peaceable death.
peaceableness: The state of being peaceable; quietness.
peaceably: Without war; without tumult or commotion; without private feuds and quarrels.
Without disturbance; quietly; without agitation; without interruption.
peacebreaker: One that violates or disturbs public peace.
peaceful: Quiet; undisturbed; not in a state of war or commotion; as a peaceful time; a peaceful country.
Pacific; mild; calm; as peaceful words; a peaceful temper.
Removed from noise or tumult; still; undisturbed; as the peaceful cottage; the peaceful scenes of rural life.
peacefully: Quietly; without disturbance. Our loved earth, where peacefully we slept.
peacefulness: Quiet; freedom from war, tumult, disturbance or discord.
Freedom from mental perturbation; as peacefulness of mind.
peacemaker: One who makes peace by reconciling parties that are at variance. Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called the children of God. Matthew 5:9.
peace-offering: An offering that procures peace. Among the Jews, an offering or sacrifice to God for atonement and reconciliation for a crime or offense.
peace-officer: A civil officer whose duty is to preserve the public peace, to prevent or punish riots; as a sheriff, or constable.
peace-parted: Dismissed from the world in peace.
peach: A tree and its fruit, of the genus Amygdalus, of many varieties. This is a delicious fruit, the produce of warm or temperate climates. In America, the peach thrives and comes to perfection in the neighborhood of Boston, northward of which it usually fails.
peach-color: The pale red color of the peach blossom.
peach-colored: Of the color of a peach blossom.
peachick: The chicken or young of the peacock.
peach-tree: The tree that produces the peach.
peacock: A large and beautiful fowl of the genus Pavo, properly the male of the species, but in usage the name is applied to the species in general. The feathers of this fowl’s tail are very long, and variegated with rich and elegant colors. The peacock is a native of India.
peacock-fish: A fish of the Indian seas, having streaks of beautiful colors.
peahen: The hen or female of the peacock.
peak: The top of a hill or mountain, ending in a point; as the peak of Teneriffe.
A point; the end of any thing that terminates in a point.
The upper corner of a sail which is extended by a gaff or yard; also, the extremity of the yard or gaff.
To make a mean figure; to sneak.
peakish: Denoting or belonging to an acuminated situation.
peal: A loud sound, usually a succession of loud sounds, as of bells, thunder, cannon, shouts of a multitude.
To utter loud and solemn sounds; as the pealing organ.
To assail with noise. Nor was his ear less pealed.
To cause to ring or sound; to celebrate. The warriors name . Though pealed and chimed on all the tongues of fame.
pealing: Uttering a loud sound or successive sounds; resounding.
peanism: The song or shouts of praise or of battle; shouts of triumph.
pear: The fruit of the Pyrus communis, of many varieties, some of which are delicious to the taste.
peck: The fourth part of a bushel; a dry measure of eight quarts; as a peck of wheat or oats.
In low language, a great deal; as, to be in a peck of troubles.
To strike with the beak; to thrust the beak into, as a bird that pecks a hole in a tree.
To strike with a pointed instrument, or to delve or dig with any thing pointed, as with a pick-ax.
To pick up food with the beak.
pecked: Struck or penetrated with a beak or pointed instrument.
pecker: One that pecks; a bird that pecks holes in trees; a woodpecker.
pecking: Striking with the bill; thrusting the beak into; thrusting into with a pointed instrument; taking up food with the beak.
pectinal: Pertaining to a comb; resembling a comb.
A fish whose bones resemble the teeth of a comb.
pectinate: Having resemblance to the teeth of a comb. In botany, a pectinate leaf is a sort of pinnate leaf, in which the leaflets are toothed like a comb. A mineral is pectinated, when it presents short filaments, crystals or branches, nearly parallel and equidistant.
pectinated: Having resemblance to the teeth of a comb. In botany, a pectinate leaf is a sort of pinnate leaf, in which the leaflets are toothed like a comb. A mineral is pectinated, when it presents short filaments, crystals or branches, nearly parallel and equidistant.
pectination: A combing; the combing of the head.
pectinite: A fossil pecten or scallop, or scallop petrified.
pectoral: Pertaining to the breast; as the pectoral muscles; pectoral medicines. The pectoral fins of a fish are situated on the sides of the fish, behind the gills.
A sacerdotal habit or vestment worn by the Jewish high priest, called in our version of the Bible, a breastplate.
A medicine adapted to cure or relieve complaints of the breast and lungs.
peculate: To defraud the public of money or goods entrusted to one’s care, by appropriating the property to one’s own use; to defraud by embezzlement.
peculation: The act, practice or crime of defrauding the public by appropriating to one’s own use the money or goods entrusted to one’s care for management or disbursement; embezzlement of public money or goods.
peculator: One that defrauds the public by appropriating to his own use money entrusted to his care.
peculiar: Appropriate; belonging to a person and to him only. Almost every writer has a peculiar style. Most men have manners peculiar to themselves.
Singular; particular. The man has something peculiar in his deportment.
Particular; special. My fate is Juno’s most peculiar care.
Belonging to a nation, system or other thing, and not to others.
Exclusive property; that which belongs to a person in exclusion of others.
peculiarity: Something peculiar to a person or thing; that which belongs to or is found in one person or thing and in no other; as a peculiarity of style or manner of thinking; peculiarity in dress.
peculiarly: In a manner not common to others.
peculiarness: The state of being peculiar; appropriation.
pecuniary: Relating to money; as pecuniary affairs or losses.
Consisting of money; as a pecuniary mulct or penalty.
pedagogic: Suiting or belonging to a teacher of children or to a pedagogue.
pedagogical: Suiting or belonging to a teacher of children or to a pedagogue.
pedagogism: The business, character or manners of a pedagogue.
pedagogue: A teacher of children; one whose occupation is to instruct young children; a schoolmaster.
To teach with the air of a pedagogue; to instruct superciliously.
pedagogy: Instruction in the first rudiments; preparatory discipline.
pedal: One of the large pipes of an organ, so called because played and stopped with the foot.
pedant: A person who makes a vain display of his learning.
pedantic: Ostentatious of learning; vainly displaying or making a show of knowledge; applied to persons or things; as a pedantic writer or scholar; a pedantic description or expression.
pedantical: Ostentatious of learning; vainly displaying or making a show of knowledge; applied to persons or things; as a pedantic writer or scholar; a pedantic description or expression.
pedantically: With a vain or boastful display of learning.
pedantize: To play the pedant; to domineer over lads; to use pedantic expressions.
pedantry: Vain ostentation of learning; a boastful display of knowledge of any kind. Horace has enticed me into this pedantry of quotation. Pedantry is the unseasonable ostentation of learning.
pedarian: A Roman senator who gave his vote by the feet, that is, by walking over to the side he espoused, in divisions of the senate.
pedate: In botany, divided like the toes. A pedate leaf is one in which a bifid petiole connects several leaflets on the inside only.
pedatifid: A pedatifid leaf, in botany, is one whose parts are not entirely separate, but connected like the toes of a water-fowl.
peddle: To travel about the country and retail goods. He peddles for a living.
To sell or retail, usually by traveling about the country.
peddling: Traveling about and selling small wares.
pedestal: In architecture, the lowest part of a column or pillar; the part which sustains a column or serves as its foot. It consists of three parts, the base, the die and the cornice.
pedestrian: Going on foot; walking; made on foot; as a pedestrian journey.
One that walks or journeys on foot.
One that walks for a wager; a remarkable walker.
pedicel: In botany, the ultimate division of a common peduncle; the stalk that supports one flower only when there are several on a peduncle.
pedicle: In botany, the ultimate division of a common peduncle; the stalk that supports one flower only when there are several on a peduncle.
pedicellate: Having a pedicel, or supported by a pedicel.
pedigree: Lineage; line of ancestors from which a person or tribe descends; genealogy. Alterations of surnames--have obscured the truth of our pedigrees.
An account or register of a line of ancestors. The Jews preserved the pedigrees of their several tribes.
pediluvy: The bathing of the feet; a bath for the feet.
pediment: In architecture, an ornament that crowns the ordinances, finishes the fronts of buildings and serves as a decoration over gates, windows and niches. It is of two forms, triangular and circular. A pediment is properly the representation of the roof.
pedler: A traveling foot-trader; one that carries about small commodities on his back, or in a cart or wagon, and sells them.
pedlery: Small wares sold or carried about for sale by pedlers.
pedobaptism: The baptism of infants or of children.
pedobaptist: One that holds to infant baptism; one that practices the baptism of children. Most denominations of christians are pedobaptists.
pedometer: An instrument by which paces are numbered as a person walks, and the distance from place to place ascertained. It also marks the revolutions of wheels. This is done by means of wheels with teeth and a chain or string fastened to the foot or to the wheel of a carriage; the wheels advancing a notch at every step or at every revolution of the carriage wheel.
pedometrical: Pertaining to or measured by a pedometer.
peduncle: In botany, the stem or stalk that supports the fructification of a plant, and of course the fruit.
peduncular: Pertaining to a peduncle; growing from a peduncle; as a peduncular tendril.
pedunculate: Growing on a peduncle; as a pedunculate flower.
peek: in our popular dialect, is the same as peep, to look through a crevice.
peel: To strip off skin, bark or rind without a cutting instrument; to strip by drawing or tearing off the skin; to bark; to flay; to decorticate. When a knife is used, we call it paring. Thus we say, to peel a tree, to peel an orange; but we say, to pare an apple to pare land.
In a general sense, to remove the skin, bark or rind, even with an instrument.
To strip; to plunder; to pillage; as, to peel a province or conquered people.
The skin or rind of any thing; as the peel of an orange.
A kind of wooden shovel used by bakers, with a broad palm and long handle; hence, in popular use in America, any large fire-shovel.
peeled: Stripped of skin, bark or rind; plundered; pillaged.
peeler: One that peels, strips or flays.
peeling: Stripping off skin or bark; plundering.
peep: To begin to appear; to make the first appearance; to issue or come forth from concealment, as through a narrow avenue./ . I can see his pride . Peep through each part of him. When flowers first peeped-- .
To look through a crevice; to look narrowly, closely or slyly. A fool will peep in at the door. Thou are a maid and must not peep.
To cry, as chickens; to utter a fine shrill sound, as through a crevice; usually written pip, but without reason, as it is the same word as is here defined, and in America is usually pronounced peep.
First appearance; as the peep of day.
A sly look, or a look through a crevice.
peeper: A chicken just breaking the shell.
peep-hole: A hole or crevice through which one may peep or look without being discovered.
peeping-hole: A hole or crevice through which one may peep or look without being discovered.
peer: An equal; one of the same rank. A man may be familiar with his peers.
An equal in excellence or endowments. In song he never had his peer.
A companion; a fellow; an associate. He all his peers in beauty did surpass.

Anobleman; as a peer of the realm; the house of peers, so called because noblemen and barons were originally considered as the companions of the king, like L. comesIn England, persons belonging to the five degrees of nobility are all peers.
To come just in sight; to appear; a poetic word. So honor peereth in the meanest habit. See how his gorget peers above his gown.
peerage: The rank or dignity of a peer or nobleman.
peeress: The consort of a peer; a noble lady.
peerless: Unequaled; having no peer or equal; as peerless beauty or majesty.
peerlessness: The state of having no equal.
peevish: Fretful; petulant; apt to mutter and complain; easily vexed or fretted; querulous; hard to please. She is peevish, sullen.
Expressing discontent and fretfulness. I will not presume . To send such peevish tokens to a king.
peevishly: Fretfully; petulantly; with discontent and murmuring.
peevishness: Fretfulness; petulance; disposition to murmur; sourness of temper; as childish peevishness. When peevishness and spleen succeed.
peg: A small pointed piece of wood used in fastening boards or other work of woodIt does the office of a nail. The word is applied only to small pieces of wood pointed; to the larger pieces thus pointed we give the name of pins, and pins in ship carpentry are called tree-nails or trenails. Coxe, in his travels in Russia, speaks of poles or beams fastened into the ground with pegs.
The pins of an instrument on which the strings are strained.
A nickname for Margaret. To take a peg lower, to depress; to lower.
pegm: A sort of moving machine in the old pageants.
pegmatite: Primitive granitic rock, composed essentially of lamellar feldspar and quartz; frequently with a mixture of mica. In it are found kaolin, tin tourmaline, beryl, aqua marina, tantale, scheelin and other valuable minerals.
peirastic: Treating of or representing trials or attempts; as the peirastic dialogues of Plato.
pelage: The vesture or covering of wild beasts, consisting of hair, fur or wool.
pelagian: Pertaining to the sea; as pelagian shells.
A follower of Pelagius, a monk of Banchor or Bangor, who denied original sin, and asserted the doctrine of free will and the merit of good works.
Pertaining to Pelagius and his doctrines.
pelagic: Pertaining to the sea; as pelagian shells.
pelf: Money; riches; but it often conveys the idea of something ill gotten or worthless. It has no plural.
pelican: A fowl of the genus Pelicanus. It is larger than the swan, and remarkable for its enormous bill, to the lower edges of the under chop of which is attached a pouch or bag, capable of being distended so as to hold many quarts of water. In this bag the fowl deposits the fish is takes for food.

A chimical glass vessel or alembic with a tubulated capital, from which two opposite and crooked beaks pass out and enter again at the belly of the cucurbit. It is designed for continued distillation and cohobation; the volatile parts of the substance distilling, rising into the capital and returning through the beaks into the cucurbit.
peliom: A mineral, a variety of iolite.
pelisse: pelee’s. Originally, a furred robe or coat. But the name is now given to a silk coat or habit worn by ladies.
pell: A skin or hide. Clerk of the pells, in England, an officer of the exchequer, who enters every teller’s bill on the parchment rolls, the roll or receipts and the roll of disbursements.
pellet: A little ball; as a pellet of wax or lint.
A bullet; a ball for fire-arms.
pellicle: Among chimists, a thin saline crust formed on the surface of a solution of salt evaporated to a certain degree. This pellicle consists of saline particles crystallized.
pellitory: The name of several plants of different genera. The pellitory of the wall or common pellitory is of the genus Parietaria; the bastard pellitory of the genus Achillea; and the pellitory of Spain is the Anthemis pyrethrum.
pellucid: Perfectly clear; transparent; not opake; as a body as pellucid as crystal.
pellucidity: Perfect clearness; transparency; as the pellucidity of the air; the pellucidness of a gem.
pellucidness: Perfect clearness; transparency; as the pellucidity of the air; the pellucidness of a gem.
pelt: The skin of a beast with the hair on it; a raw hide.
The quarry of a hawk all torn.
A blow or stroke from something thrown.
Properly, to strike with something thrown, driven or falling; as, to pelt with stones; pelted with hail. The chiding billows seem to pelt the clouds.
peltate: In botany, having the shape of a target or round shield, as a peltate stigma; having the petiole inserted in the disk, as a peltate leaf.
peltated: In botany, having the shape of a target or round shield, as a peltate stigma; having the petiole inserted in the disk, as a peltate leaf.
peltately: In the form of a target.
pelted: Struck with something thrown or driven.
pelter: One that pelts; also, a pinch-penny; a mean, sordid person.
pelting: Striking with something thrown or driven.
An assault with any thing thrown.
pelt-monger: A dealer in pelts or raw hides.
peltry: The skins of animals producing fur; skins in general, with the fur on them; furs in general.
pelvimeter: An instrument to measure the dimensions of the female pelvis.
pelvis: The cavity of the body formed by the os sacrum, os coccyx, and ossa innominata, forming the lower part of the abdomen.
pen: An instrument used for writing, usually made of the quill of some large fowl, but it may be of any other material.
pret. and pp. penned. To write; to compose and commit to paper.
A small inclosure for beasts, as for cows or sheep.
pret. and pp. penned or pent. To shut in a pen; to confine in a small inclosure; to coop; to confine in a narrow place, usually followed by up, which is redundant.
penal: Enacting punishment; denouncing the punishment of offenses; as a penal law or statute; the penal code. Penal statutes must be construed strictly.
Inflicting punishment. Adamantine chains and penal fire.
Incurring punishment; subject to a penalty; as a penal act of offense.
penalty: The suffering in person or property which is annexed by law or judicial decision to the commission of a crime, offense or trespass, as a punishment. A fine is a pecuniary penalty. The usual penalties inflicted on the person, are whipping, cropping, branding, imprisonment, hard labor, transportation or death.

The suffering to which a person subjects himself by covenant or agreement, in case of non-fulfillment of his stipulations; the forfeiture or sum to be forfeited for non-payment, or for non- compliance with an agreement; as the penalty of a bond.
penance: The suffering, labor or pain to which a person voluntarily subjects himself, or which is imposed on him by authority as a punishment for his faults, or as an expression of penitence; such as fasting, flagellation, wearing chainsPenance is one of the seven sacraments of the Romish church.
pence: The plural of penny, when used of a sum of money or value. When pieces of coin are mentioned, we use pennies.
pencil: A small brush used by painters for laying on colors. The proper pencils are made of fine hair or bristles, as of camels, badgers or squirrels, or of the down of swans, inclosed in a quill. The larger pencils, made of swine’s bristles, are called brushes.
A pen formed of carburet of iron or plumbago, black lead or red chalk, with a point at one end, used for writing and drawing.
Any instrument of writing without ink.
An aggregate or collection of rays of light.
To paint or draw; to write or mark with a pencil.
penciled: Painted, drawn or marked with a pencil.
penciling: Painting, drawing or marking with a pencil.
pencil-shaped: Having the shape of a pencil.
pendant: An ornament or jewel hanging at the ear, usually composed of pearl or some precious stone.
Any thing hanging by way of ornament.
In heraldry, a part hanging from the label, resembling the drops in the Doric frieze.

A streamer; a small flag or long narrow banner displayed from a ship’s mast head, usually terminating in two points called the swallow’s tail. It denotes that a ship is in actual service. The broad pendant is used to distinguish the chief of a squadron.

A short piece of rope fixed on each side under the shrouds, on the heads of the main and fore-mast, having an iron thimble to receive the hooks of the tackle. There are many other pendants consisting of a rope or ropes, to whose lower extremity is attached a block or tackle. The rudder- pendant is a rope made fast to the rudder by a chain, to prevent the loss of the rudder when unshipped.
pendency: Suspense; the state of being undecided; as, to wait during the pendency of a suit or petition.
pendent: Hanging; fastened at one end, the other being loose. With ribbons pendent, flaring about her head.
Jutting over; projecting; as a pendant rock.
pending: Depending; remaining undecided; not terminated. This was done, pending the suit.
pendulous: Hanging; swinging; fastened to one end, the other being movable. The dewlap of an animal is pendulous.
pendulum: A vibrating body suspended from a fixed point; as the pendulum of a clock. The oscillations of a pendulum depend on gravity, and are always performed in nearly equal times, supposing the length of the pendulum and the gravity to remain the same.
penetrability: Susceptibility of being penetrated, or of being entered or passed through by another body. There being no mean between penetrability and impenetrability: .
penetrable: That may be penetrated, entered, or pierced by another body. Let him try thy dart, And pierce his only penetrable part.
Susceptible of moral or intellectual impression. I am not made of stone, But penetrable to your kind entreaties.
penetrancy: Power of entering or piercing; as the penetrancy of subtil effluvia.
penetrant: Having the power to enter or pierce; sharp; subtil; as penetrant spirit; food subtilized and rendered fluid and penetrant.
penetrate: To enter or pierce; to make way into another body; as, a sword or dart penetrates the body; oil penetrates wood; marrow, the most penetrating of oil substances.
To affect the mind; to cause to feel. I am penetrated with a lively sense of your generosity.
To reach by the intellect; to understand; as, to penetrate the meaning or design of any thing.
To enter; to pass into the interior; as, to penetrate a country.
To pass; to make way. Born where heaven’s influence scarce can penetrate.
penetrating: a. Having the power of entering or piercing another body; sharp; subtil. Oil is a penetrating substance.
Acute; discerning; quick to understand; as a penetrating mind.
penetration: The act of entering a body.
Mental entrance into any thing abstruse; as a penetration into the abstruse difficulties of algebra.
Acuteness; sagacity; as a man of great or nice penetration.
penetrative: Piercing; sharp; subtil. Let not air be too gross nor too penetrative.
Acute; sagacious; discerning; as penetrative wisdom.
Having the power to affect or impress the mind; as penetrative shame.
penfish: A kind of eelpout with a smooth skin.
penguin: A genus of fowls of the order of Palmipeds. The penguin is an aquatic fowl with very short legs, with four toes, three of which are webbed; the body is clothed with short feathers, set as compactly as the scales of a fish; the wings are small like fins, and covered with short scale-like feathers, so that they are useless in flight. Penguins seldom go on shore, except in the season of breeding, when they burrow like rabbits. On land they stand erect; they are tame and may be driven like a flock of sheep. In water they swim with rapidity, being assisted by their wings. These fowls are found only in the southern latitudes.
penicil: Among physicians, a tent or pledget for wounds or ulcers.
peninsula: A portion of land, connected with a continent by a narrow neck or isthmus, but nearly surrounded with water. Thus Boston stands on a peninsula.
A large extent of country joining the main land by a part narrower than the tract itself. Thus Spain and Portugal are said to be situated on a peninsula.
peninsular: In the form or state of a peninsula; pertaining to a peninsula.
peninsulate: To encompass almost with water; to form a peninsula. South river peninsulates Castle hill farm, and at high tides, surrounds it.
penitence: Repentance; pain; sorrow or grief of heart for sins or offenses; contrition. Real penitence springs from a conviction of guilt and ingratitude to God, and is followed by amendment of life.
penitency: Repentance; pain; sorrow or grief of heart for sins or offenses; contrition. Real penitence springs from a conviction of guilt and ingratitude to God, and is followed by amendment of life.
penitent: Suffering pain or sorrow of heart on account of sins, crimes or offenses; contrite; sincerely affected by a sense of guilt and resolving on amendment of life. The proud he tam’d, the penitent he cheer’d.
One that repents of sin; one sorrowful on account of his transgressions.
One under church censure, but admitted to penance.

One under the direction of a confessor. Penitents is an appellation given to certain fraternities in catholic countries, distinguished by their habits and employed in charitable acts. Order of penitents, a religious order established by one Bernard of Marseilles, about the year 1272, for the reception of reformed courtezans. The congregation of penitents at Paris, was founded with a similar view.
penitential: Proceeding from or expressing penitence or contrition of heart; as penitential sorrow or tears.
Among the Romanists, a book containing the rules which relate to penance and the reconciliation of penitents.
penitentiary: Relating to penance, or to the rules and measures of penance.
One that prescribes the rules and measures of penance.
A penitent; one that does penance.
At the court of Rome, an office in which are examined and delivered out the secret bulls, graces or dispensations relating to cases of conscience, confession.
An officer in some cathedrals, vested with power from the bishop to absolve in cases reserved to him. The pope has a grand penitentiary, who is a cardinal and is chief of the other penitentiaries.
penitently: With penitence; with repentance, sorrow or contrition for sin.
penknife: A small knife used for making and mending pens.
penman: A man that professes or teaches the art of writing. More generally,
One that writes a good hand.
An author; a writer; as the sacred penmen.
penmanship: The use of the pen in writing; the art of writing.
Manner of writing; as good or bad penmanship.
pennached: Radiated; diversified with natural stripes of various colors; as a flower.
pennant: A tackle for hoisting things on board a ship.
pennon: A tackle for hoisting things on board a ship.
pennate: In botany, a pennate leaf is a compound leaf in which a simple petiole has several leaflets attached to each side of it. [See Pinnate.I .
pennated: In botany, a pennate leaf is a compound leaf in which a simple petiole has several leaflets attached to each side of it. [See Pinnate.I .
penniform: Having the form of a quill or feather.
penny: pennies or pence. Pennies denotes the number of coins; pence the amount of pennies in value.
An ancient English silver coin; but now an imaginary money of account, twelve of which are equal to a shilling. It is the radical denomination from which English coin is numbered.
In ancient English statutes, any or all silver money.
Proverbially, a small sum. He will not lend a penny.
Money in general. Be sure to turn the penny.
pennypost: One that carries letters from the post office and delivers them to the proper persons for a penny or other small compensation.
pennyroyal: A plant of the genus Mentha. The English pennyroyal is the Mentha pulegium; the N. American pennyroyal is the Cunila pulegioides.
pennyweight: A troy weight containing twenty four grains, each grain being equal in weight to a grain of wheat from the middle of the ear, well dried. It was anciently the weight of a silver penny, whence the name. Twenty penny-weights make an ounce troy.
pennywise: Saving small sums at the hazard of larger; niggardly on improper occasions.
pennyworth: As much as is bought for a penny.
Any purchase; any thing bought or sold for money; that which is worth the money given.
A good bargain; something advantageously purchased, or for less than it is worth.
pensile: Hanging; suspended; as a pensile bell.
Supported above the ground; as a pensile garden.
pension: An annual allowance of a sum of money to a person by government in consideration of past services, civil or military. Men often receive pensions for eminent services on retiring from office. But in particular, officers, soldiers and seamen receive pensions when they are disabled for further services.
An annual payment by an individual to an old or disabled servant.
In Great Britain, an annual allowance made by government to indigent widows of officers killed or dying in public service.
A yearly payment in the inns of court.
pensionary: Maintained by a pension; receiving a pension; as pensionary spies.
Consisting in a pension; as a pensionary provision for maintenance.
A person who receives a pension from government for past services, or a yearly allowance from some prince, company or individual.
The first minister of the states of the province of Holland; also, the first minister of the regency of a city in Holland.
pensioner: One to whom an annual sum of money is paid by government in consideration of past services.
One who receives an annual allowance for services.
In the university of Cambridge, and in that of Dublin, an undergraduate or bachelor of arts who lives at his own expense.

One of an honorable band of gentlemen who attend on the king of England, and receive a pension or an annual allowance of a hundred pounds. This band was instituted by Henry VII. Their duty is to guard the king’s person in his own house.
pensioning: Granting an annual allowance for past services.
pensive: Literally, thoughtful; employed in serious study or reflection; but it often implies some degree of sorrow, anxiety, depression or gloom of mind; thoughtful and sad, or sorrowful. Anxious cares the pensive nymph oppress’d.
Expressing thoughtfulness with sadness; as pensive numbers; pensive strains.
pensively: With thoughtfulness; with gloomy seriousness or some degree of melancholy.
pensiveness: Gloomy thoughtfulness; melancholy; seriousness from depressed spirits.
penstock: A narrow or confined place formed by a frame of timber planked or boarded, for holding or conducting the water of a mill-pond to a wheel, and furnished with a flood gate which may be shut or opened at pleasure.
pent: of pen. Shut up; closely confined.
pentachord: An instrument of music with five strings.z
An order or system of five sounds.
pentacoccous: Having or containing five grains or seeds, or having five cells with one seed in each.
pentacoster: In ancient Greece, a military officer commanding fifty men; but the number varied.
pentacostys: A body of fifty soldiers; but the number varied.
pentacrinite: The fossil remains of a zoophyte.
pentacrostic: Containing five acrostics of the same name in five divisions of each verse.
A set of verses so disposed as to have five acrostics of the same name in five divisions of each verse.
pentadactyl: In botany, a plant called five fingers; a name given to the Ricinus or Palma Christi, from the shape of its leaf.
In ichthyology, the five fingered fish; a name given to a fish common in the East Indian seas, which has five black streaks on each side resembling the prints of five fingers.
pentagon: In geometry, a figure of five sides and five angles.
In fortification, a fort with five bastions.
pentagraph: An instrument for drawing figures in any proportion at pleasure, or for copying or reducing a figure, plan, printto any desired size.
pentagraphic: Pertaining to a pentagraph; performed by a pentagraph.
pentagraphical: Pertaining to a pentagraph; performed by a pentagraph.
pentagyn: In botany, a plant having five pistils.
pentahedron: A figure having five equal sides.
pentahexahedral: In crystalography, exhibiting five ranges of faces one above another, each range containing six faces.
pentameter: In ancient poetry, a verse of five feet. The two first feet may be either dactyls or spondees; the third is always a spondee, and the two last anapests. A pentameter verse subjoined to a hexameter, constitutes what is called elegiac.
pentander: In botany, a plant having five stamens.
pentapetalous: Having five petals or flower leaves.
pentarchy: A government in the hands of five persons.
pentastich: A composition consisting of five verses.
pentastyle: In architecture, a work containing five rows of columns.
pentateuch: The first five books of the Old Testament.
penteconter: A Grecian vessel of fifty oars, smaller than a trireme.
pentecost: A solemn festival of the Jews, so called because celebrated on the fiftieth day after the sixteenth of Nisan, which was the second day of the passover. It was called the feast of weeks, because it was celebrated seven weeks after the passover. It was instituted to oblige the people to repair to the temple of the Lord, there to acknowledge his absolute dominion over the country, and offer him the first fruits of their harvest; also that they might call to mind and give thanks to God for the law which he had given them at Sinai on the fiftieth day from their departure from lEgypt.
Whitsuntide, a solemn feast of the church, held in commemoration of the descent of the Holy Spirit on the apostles. Acts 2:1.
pentecostals: Oblations formerly made by parishioners to the parish priest at the feast of Pentecost, and sometimes by inferior churches to the mother church.
penthouse: A shed standing aslope from the main wall or building.
pentile: A tile for covering the sloping part of a roof.
pentremite: A genus of zoophytes or fossil shells.
penult: The last syllable of a word except one.
penultimate: The last but one; a word used of the last syllable of a word except one. It may be sometimes used as a noun.
penumbra: In astronomy, a partial shade or obscurity on the margin of the perfect shade in an eclipse, or between the perfect shade, where the light is entirely intercepted, and the full light.
penurious: Excessively saving or sparing in the use of money; parsimonious to a fault; sordid; as a penurious man. It expresses somewhat less than niggardly.
Scanty; affording little; as a penurious’§ spring.
penuriously: In a saving or parsimonious manner; with scanty supply.
peruriousness: Parsimony; a sordid disposition to save money.
penury: Want of property; indigence; extreme poverty. All innocent they were exposed to hardship and penury.
peon: In Hindoostan, a foot soldier, or a footman armed with sword and target; said to be corrupted from piadah. Hence,
In France, a common man in chess; usually written and called pawn.
peony: A plant and flower of the genus Paeonia. It is written also piony.
An herbaceous perennial plant of the genus Paeonia, with tuberous roots, and bearing large beautiful red flowers. PION Y.
people: The body of persons who compose a community, town, city or nation. We say, the people of a town; the people of London or Paris; the English people. In this sense, the word is not used in the plural, but it comprehends all classes of inhabitants, considered as a collective body, or any portion of the inhabitants of a city or country.
The vulgar; the mass of illiterate persons. The knowing artist may judge better than the people.
The commonalty, as distinct from men of rank. Myself shall mount the rostrum in his favor, And strive to gain his pardon from the people.
Persons of a particular class; a part of a nation or community; as country people.
Persons in general; any persons indefinitely; like on in French, and man in Saxon. People were tempted to lend by great premiums and large interest.
pepastic: A medicine that serves to help digestion; applied particularly to such medicines as tend to promote the digestion of wounds.
pepper: To sprinkle with pepper. -BOxXA small box with a perforated lid, used for sprinkling pulverized pepper on food.

A plant and its seed or grain, of the genus Piper. The stem of the plant is a vine requiring a prop, which is usually a tree. The leaves are oval and the flower white. We have three kinds of pepper, the black, the white, and the long. The black pepper is the produce of Java, Sumatra, Ceylon, and other Asiatic countries; The white pepper is the black pepper decorticated; the long pepper is the fruit of a different species, also from the E. Indies. It consists of numerous grains attached to a common footstalk. Pepper has a strong aromatic smell and a pungent taste.
To beat; to pelt with shot; to mangle with blows.
pepper-cake: A kind of spiced cake or gingerbread.
pepper-corn: The berry or seed of the pepper-plant.
Something of inconsiderable value; as lands held at the rent of a pepper-corn.
pepper-gingerbread: A kind of cake made in England.
peppergrass: A plant of the genus Pilularia; also, a plant of the genus Lepidium.
peppermint: A plant of the genus Mentha. It is aromatic and pungent. Also, a liquor distilled from the plant.
peppermint-tree: The Eucalyptus piperita, a native of New South Wales.
pepper-pot: A plant of the genus Capsicum.
pepper-tree: A plant of the venus Vitis.
pepper-water: A liquor prepared from powdered black pepper; used in microscopical observations.
pepper-wort: A plant of the genus Lepidium.
peptic: Promoting digestion; dietetic, as peptic precepts.
per: a Latin preposition, denoting through, passing, or over the whole extent, as in perambulo. Hence it is sometimes equivalent to very in English, as in peracutus, very sharp. As a prefix, in English, it retains these significations, and in chimistry it is used to denote very of fully, to the utmost extent, as in peroxyd, a substance oxydated to the utmost degree. Per is used also for by, as per bearer, by the bearer. Per annum, by the year; in each year successively. Per se, by himself; by itself; by themselves.
peracute: Very sharp; very violent; as a peracute fever.
peradventure: By chance; perhaps; it may be. It has been used as a noun for doubt or question, but rather improperly. The word is obsolescent and inelegant.
peragrate: To travel over or through; to wander; to ramble.
peragration: The act of passing through any space; as the peragration of the moon in her monthly revolution.
perambulate: To walk through or over; properly and technically, to pass through or over for the purpose of surveying or examining something; to visit as overseers; as, to perambulate a parish. So in New England, the laws require the select men of towns to appoint suitable persons annually to perambulate the borders or bounds of the township, and renew the boundaries, or see that the old ones are in a good state.
perambulating: Passing over or through for the purpose of inspection.
perambulation: The act of passing or walking through or over.
A district within which a person has the right of inspection; jurisdiction.
Annual survey of the bounds of a parish in England, or of a township in America.
perambulator: An instrument or wheel for measuring distances, to be used in surveying or traveling; called also a pedometer.
perbisulphate: A sulphate with two proportions of sulphuric acid, and combined with an oxyd at the maximum of oxydation.
percarbureted: The percarbureted hydrogen of the French chimists is said to be the only definite compound of these two elements.
perceivable: Perceptible; that may be perceived; that may fall under perception or the cognizance of the senses; that may be felt, seen, heard, smelt or tasted. We say, the roughness of cloth is perceivable; the dawn of the morning is perceivable; the sound of a bell is perceivable; the scent of an orange is perceivable; the difference of taste in an apple and an orange is perceivable.
That may be known, understood or conceived.
perceivably: In such a manner as to be perceived.
perceive: To have knowledge or receive impressions of external objects through the medium or instrumentality of the senses or bodily organs; as, to perceive light or color; to perceive the cold or ice or the taste of honey.
To know; to understand; to observe. Till we ourselves see it with our own eyes, and perceive it by our own understanding, we are in the dark.
To be affected by; to receive impressions from. The upper regions of the air perceive the collection of the matter of tempests before the air below.
perceived: Known by the senses; felt; understood; observed.
perceiver: One who perceives, feels or observes.
perceptibility: The state or quality of being perceptible; as the perceptibility of light or color.
perceptible: That may be perceived; that may impress the bodily organs; that may come under the cognizance of the senses; as a perceptible degree of heat or cold; a perceptible odor; a perceptible sound. A thing may be too minute to be perceptible to the touch.
That may be known or conceived of.
perceptibly: In a manner to be perceived. The §= woman decays perceptibly every week.
perception: The act of perceiving or of receiving impressions by the senses; or that act or process of the mind which makes known an external object. In other words, the notice which the mind takes of external objects. We gain a knowledge of the coldness and smoothness of marble by perception.
In philosophy, the faculty of perceiving; the faculty or peculiar part of man’s constitution, by which he has knowledge through the medium or instrumentality of the bodily organs.
The state of being affected or capable of being affected by something external. This experiment discovers perception in plants.
perceptivity: The power of perception of thinking.
perch: A fish of the genus Perca. This fish has a deep body, very rough scales, an arched back, and prickly dorsal fins.
A pole; hence, a roost for fowls, which is often a pole; also, any thing on which they light.

A measure of length containing five yards and a half; a rod. In the popular language of America, rod is chiefly used; but rod, pole, and perch, all signifying the same thing, may be used indifferently.
To sit or roost; as a bird.
To light or settle on a fixed body; as a bird.
perchers: Paris candles anciently used in England; also, a larger sort of wax candles which were usually set on the altar.
perchlorate: A compound of perchloric acid with a base.
perchloric: Perchloric acid is chlorine converted into an acid by combining with a maximum of oxygen.
percipient: Perceiving; having the faculty of perception. Animals are percipient beings; mere matter is not percipient.
One that perceives or has the faculty of perception.
percolate: To strain through; to cause to pass through small interstices, as a liquor; to filter.
To pass through small interstices; to filter; as, water percolates through a porous stone.
percolation: The act of straining or filtering; filtration; the act of passing through small interstices, as liquor through felt or a porous stone. Percolation is intended for the purification of liquors.
percussion: The act of striking one body against another, with some violence; as the vibrations excited in the air by percussion.
The impression one body makes on another by falling on it or striking it.
The impression or effect of sound on the ear.
percutient: That which strikes, or has power to strike.
perdifoil: A plant that annually loses or drops its leaves; opposed to evergreen. The passion flower of America and the jasmine of Malabar, which are evergreens in their native climates, become perdifoils when transplanted into Britain.
perdition: Entire loss or ruin; utter destruction; as the perdition of the Turkish fleet.

The utter loss of the soul or of final happiness in a future state; future misery or eternal death. The impenitent sinner is condemned to final perdition. If we reject the truth, we seal our own perdition.
perdu: Close; in concealment. The moderator, out of view, Beneath the desk had lain perdue.
One that is placed on the watch or in ambush.
Abandoned; employed on desperate purposes; accustomed to desperate purposes or enterprises.
perdue: Close; in concealment. The moderator, out of view, Beneath the desk had lain perdue.
peregrinate: To travel from place to place or from one country to another; to live in a foreign country.
peregrination: A traveling from one country to another; a wandering; abode in foreign countries.
peregrine: Foreign; not native. Peregrine falcon, a species of hawk, the black hawk or falcon, found in America and in Asia, and which wanders in summer to the Arctic circle.
perempt: In law, to kill; to crush or destroy.
peremptorily: Absolutely; positively; in a decisive manner; so as to preclude further debate. Never judge peremptorily on first appearances.
peremptoriness: Positiveness; absolute decision; dogmatism. Peremptoriness is of two sorts; one, a magisterialness in matters of opinion; the other, a positiveness in matters of fact.
peremptory: Express; positive; absolute; decisive; authoritative; in a manner to preclude debate or expostulation. The orders of the commander are peremptory.
Positive in opinion or judgment. The genuine effect of sound learning is to make men less’- peremptory in their determinations.
Peremptory challenge, in law, a challenge or right of challenging jurors without showing cause.
perennial: Lasting or continuing without cessation through the year.
