physiognomics: Among physicians, signs in the countenance which indicate the state, temperament or constitution of the body and mind.
physiognomist: One that is skilled in physiognomy; one that is able to judge of the particular temper or other qualities of the mind, by signs in the countenance.
physiognomy: The art or science of discerning the character of the mind from the features of the face; or the art of discovering the predominant temper or other characteristic qualities of the mind by the form of the body, but especially by the external signs of the countenance, or the combination of the features.
The face or countenance with respect to the temper of the mind; particular configuration, cast or expression of countenance.
physiography: A description of nature, or the science of natural objects.
physiologic: Pertaining to physiology; relating to the science of the properties and functions of living beings.
physiological: Pertaining to physiology; relating to the science of the properties and functions of living beings.
physiologically: According to the principles of physiology.
physiologist: One who is versed in the science of living beings, or in the properties and functions of animals and plants.
physiology: According to the Greek, this word signifies a discourse or treatise of nature, but the moderns use the word in a more limited sense, for the science of the properties and functions of animals and plants, comprehending what is common to all animals and plants, and what is peculiar to individuals and species.
The science of the mind, of its various phenomena, affections and powers.
phytivorous: Feeding on plants or herbage; as phytivorous animals.
phytographical: Pertaining to the description of plants.
phytolite: A plant petrified, or fossil vegetable.
phytologist: One versed in plants, or skilled in phytology; a botanist.
phytology: A discourse or treatise of plants, or the doctrine of plants; description of the kinds and properties of plants. Pia mater, in anatomy, a thin membrane immediately investing the brain.
piaba: A small fresh water fish of Brazil, about the size of the minnow, much esteemed for food.
pianist: A performer on the forte-piano, or one well skilled in it.
piano-forte: A keyed musical instrument of German origin and of the harpsichord kind, but smaller; so called from its softer notes or expressions. Its tones are produced by hammers instead of quills, like the virginal and spinet.
piaster: An Italian coin of about 80 cents value, or 3s. 7d. sterling. But the value is different in different states or countries. It is called also, a piece of eight.
piazza: In building, a portico or covered walk supported by arches or columns.
pib-corn: Among the Welsh, a wind instrument or pipe with a horn at each end.
pibroch: A wild irregular species of music, peculiar to the Highlands of Scotland. It is performed on a bagpipe, and adapted to excite or assuage passion, and particularly to rouse a martial spirit among troops going to battle.
pica: In ornithology, the pie or mag-pie, a species of Corvus.
In medicine, a vitiated appetite which makes the patient crave what is unfit for food, as chalk, ashes, coal.
A printing type of a large size; probably named from litera picata, a great black letter at the beginning of some new order in the liturgy; hence,

Pica, pye or pie, formerly an ordinary, a table or directory for devotional services; also, an alphabetical catalogue of names and things in rolls and records. Pica marina, the sea-pye, ostralegus, or oyster-catcher; an aquatic fowl of the genus Haematopus. This fowl feeds on oysters, limpets and marine insects.
picaroon: A plunderer; a pirate. This word is not applied to a highway robber, but to pirates and plunderers of wrecks. In all wars, Corsica and Majorca have been nests. of picaroons.
piccadil: A high collar or a kind of ruff.
piccadilly: A high collar or a kind of ruff.
pickardil: A high collar or a kind of ruff.
piccage: Money paid at fairs for breaking ground for booths.
pick: To pull off or pluck with the fingers something that grows or adheres to another thing; to separate by the hand, as fruit from trees; as, to pick apples or oranges; to pick strawberries.
To pull off or separate with the teeth, beak or claws; as, to pick flesh from a bone; hence,
To clean by the teeth, fingers or claws, or by a small instrument, by separating something that adheres; as, to pick a bone; to pick the ears.
To take up; to cause or seek industriously; as, to pick a quarrel.
To separate or pull asunder; to pull into small parcels by the fingers; to separate locks for loosening and cleaning; as, to pick wool.
pickax: An ax with a sharp point at tone end and a broad blade at the other.
picked: Plucked off by the fingers, teeth or claws; cleaned by picking; opened by an instrument; selected.
Pointed; sharp. Let the stake be made picked at the top.
piked: Pointed; sharp. Let the stake be made picked at the top.
pickedness: State of being pointed at the end; sharpness.
pickeer: To skirmish, as soldiers on the outposts of an army, or in pillaging parties.
picker: A pickax or instrument for picking or separating.
One that excites a quarrel between himself and another.
pickerel: A small pike, a fish of the genus Esox.
pickerel-weed: A plant supposed to breed pickerels.
picket: A stake sharpened or pointed; used in fortification and encampments.
A narrow board pointed; used in making fence.
A guard posted in front of an army to give notice of the approach of the enemy.
A punishment with consists in making the offender stand with one foot on a pointed stake.
picking: Pulling off with the fingers or teeth; selecting.
The act of plucking; selection; gathering; gleaning.
pickle: Brine; a solution of salt and water, sometimes impregnated with spices, in which flesh, fish or other substance is preserved; as pickle for beef; pickle for capers or for cucumbers; pickle for herring.
A state of condition of difficulty or disorder; a word used in ridicule or contempt. You are in a fine pickle. How cam’st thou in this pickle?
A parcel of land inclosed with a hedge.
To preserve in brine or pickle; as, to pickle herring.
pickle-herring: A merry Andrew; a zany; a buffoon.
picklock: An instrument for opening locks without the key.
picknick: An assembly where each person contributes to the entertainment.
pickpocket: One who steals from the pocket of another.
pickpurse: One that steals from the purse of another.
pickthank: An officious fellow who does what he is not desired to do, for the sake of gaining favor; a whispering parasite.
picktooth: An instrument for picking or cleaning the teeth.
pico: A peak; the pointed head of a mountain.
picrolite: A mineral composed chiefly of the carbonate of magnesia, of a green color.
picrotoxin: The bitter and poisonous principle of the Cocculus Indicus.
pict: A person whose body is painted.
pictorial: Pertaining to a painter; produced by a painter.
picture: A painting exhibiting the resemblance of any thing; a likeness drawn in colors. Pictures and shapes are but secondary objects.
The words of painters; painting. Quintilian, when he saw any well expressed image of grief, either in picture or sculpture, would usually weep.
Any resemblance or representation, either to the eye or to the understanding. Thus we say, a child is the picture of his father; the poet has drawn an exquisite picture of grief.
To paint a resemblance. Love is like a painter, who, in drawing the picture of a friend having a blemish in one eye, would picture only the other side of the face.
To represent; to form or present an ideal likeness. I do picture it in my mind.
pictured: Painted in resemblance; drawn in colors; represented.
picturesque: Expressing that peculiar kind of beauty which is agreeable in a picture, natural or artificial; striking the mind with great power or pleasure in representing objects of vision, and in painting to the imagination any circumstance or event as clearly as if delineated in a picture.
picturesk: Expressing that peculiar kind of beauty which is agreeable in a picture, natural or artificial; striking the mind with great power or pleasure in representing objects of vision, and in painting to the imagination any circumstance or event as clearly as if delineated in a picture.
piddle: To deal in trifles; to spend time in trifling objects; to attend to trivial concerns or the small parts rather than to the main.
To pick at table; to eat squeamishly or without appetite.
piddler: One who busies himself about little things.
One that eats squeamishly or without appetite.
pie: An article of food consisting of paste baked with something in it or under it, as apple, minced meat.
The magpie, a party-colored bird of the genus Corvus. It is sometimes written pye.
The old popish service book, supposed to be so called from the different color of the text and rubric, or from litera picata, a large black letter, used at the beginning of each order.
Printers’ types mixed or unsorted. Cork and pie, an adjuration by the pie or service book, and by the sacred name of the Deity corrupted.
piebald: Of various colors; diversified in color; as a piebald horse.
piece: A fragment or part of any thing separated from the whole, in any manner, by cutting, splitting, breaking or tearing; as, to cut in pieces, break in pieces, tear in pieces, pull in pieces; a piece of a rock; a piece of paper.
A part of any thing, though not separated, or separated only in idea; not the whole; a portion; as a piece of excellent knowledge.
A distinct part or quantity; a part considered by itself, or separated from the rest only by a boundary or divisional line; as a piece of land in the meadow or on the mountain.
A separate part; a thing or portion distinct from others of a like kind; as a piece of timber; a piece of cloth; a piece of paper hangings.
A composition, essay or writing of no great length; as a piece of poetry or prose; a piece of music.
pieced: Mended or enlarged by a piece or pieces.
pieceless: Not made of pieces; consisting of an entire thing.
piecemeal: In pieces; in fragments. On which it piecemeal broke.
By pieces; by little and little in succession. Piecemeal they win this acre first, then that.
Single; separate; made of parts or pieces.
pied: Variegated with spots of different colors; spotted. We now apply the word chiefly or wholly to animals which are marked with large spots of different colors. If the spots are small, we use speckled. This distinction was not formerly observed, and in some cases, pied is elegantly used to express a diversity of colors in small spots. Meadows trim with daisies pied.
piepoudre: An ancient court of record in England, incident to every fair and market, of which the steward of him who owns or has the toll, is the judge. It had jurisdiction of all causes arising in the fair or market.
pier: A mass of solid stone work for supporting an arch or the timbers of a bridge or other building.
A mass of stone work or a mole projecting into the sea, for breaking the force of the waves and making a safe harbor.
pierce: To thrust into with a pointed instrument; as, to pierce the body with a sword or spear; to pierce the side with a thorn.
To penetrate; to enter; to force a way into; as, a column of troops pierced the main body of the enemy; a shot pierced the ship.
To penetrate the heart deeply; to touch the passions; to excite or affect the passions. 7 Timothy 6:10.
To dive or penetrate into, as a secret or purpose.
pierced: pers’ed. Penetrated; entered by force; transfixed.
piercer: pers’er. An instrument that pierces, penetrates or bores.
piercing: pers’ing. Penetrating; entering, as a pointed instrument; making a way by force into another body.
Affecting deeply; as eloquence piercing the heart.
piercingly: pers’ingly. With penetrating force or effect; sharply.
piercingness: pers’ingness. The power of piercing or penetrating; sharpness; keenness.
pietism: Extremely strict devotion, or affectation of piety.
pietist: One of a sect professing great strictness and purity of life, despising learning, school theology and ecclesiastical polity, as also forms and ceremonies in religion, and giving themselves up to mystic theology. This sect sprung up among the protestants of Germany, in the latter part of the seventeenth century.
piety: Piety in principle, is a compound of veneration or reverence of the Supreme Being and love of his character, or veneration accompanied with love; and piety in practice, is the exercise of these affections in obedience to his will and devotion to his service. Piety is the only proper and adequate relief of decaying man.
piezometer: An instrument for ascertaining the compressibility of water, and the degree of such compressibility under any given weight.
pig: The young of swine, male or female.
An oblong mass of unforged iron, lead or other metal. A pig of lead is the eighth of a fother, or 250 pounds.
or i. To bring forth pigs.
pigeon: A fowl of the genus Columba, of several species, as the stock dove, the ring dove, the turtle dove, and the migratory or wild pigeon of America. The domestic pigeon breeds in a box, often attached to a building, called a dovecot or pigeon-house. The wild pigeon builds a nest on a tree in the forest.
pigeon-hole: A little apartment or division in a case for papers.
pigeon-holes: An old English game in which balls were rolled through little cavities or arches.
pigeon-pea: A plant of the genus Cytisus.
piggin: A small wooden vessel with an erect handle, used as a dipper.
pigmean: Very small; like a pigmy; as an image of pigmean size.
pigment: Paint; a preparation used by painters, dyersto impart colors to bodies.
pigmy: A dwarf; a person of very small stature; a name applied to a fabled nation said to have been devoured by cranes.
Very small in size; mean; feeble; inconsiderable.
pignoration: The act of pledging or pawning.
pignut: The ground nut, a plant of the genus Bunium; also, a tree and its fruit of the genus Juglans.
pigsney: A word of endearment to a girl.
pigtail: A cue; the hair of the head tied in the form of a pig’s tail.
pigwidgeon: A fairy; a cant word for any thing very small.
pike: A military weapon consisting of a long wooden shaft or staff, with a flat steel head pointed; called the spear. This weapon was formerly used by infantry, but its use is now limited to officers, and it is called a sponton or spontoon. Its use among soldiers is superseded by the bayonet.
A fork used in husbandry; but we now use fork or pitchfork.
Among turners, the iron sprigs used to fasten any thing to be turned.

In ichthyology, a fish of the genus Esox, so named from its long shape or from the form of its snout. It is a fresh water fish, living in deep water and very voracious, but very palatable food. The pike, the tyrant of the flood.
pikeman: A soldier armed with a pike.
pikestaff: The staff or shaft of a pike.
pikrolite: A mineral found at Taberg, in Sweden, supposed to be a variety of serpentine.
pilaster: A square column, sometimes insulated; but usually pilasters are set within a wall, projecting only one quarter of their diameter. Their bases, capitals and entablatures have the same parts as those of columns.
pilch: A furred gown or case; something lined with fur.
pilchard: A fish resembling the herring, but thicker and rounder; the nose is shorter and turns up; the under jaw is shorter; the back more elevated, and the belly less sharp. These fishes appear on the Cornish coast in England, about the middle of July, in immense numbers, and furnish a considerable article of commerce.
pile: A heap; a mass or collection of things in a roundish or elevated form; as a pile of stones; a pile of bricks; a pile of wood or timber; a pile of ruins.
A collection of combustibles for burning a dead body; as a funeral pile.
A large building or mass of buildings; an edifice. The pile o’erlook’d the town and drew the sight.
A heap of balls or shot laid in horizontal courses, rising into a pyramidical form.

A large stake or piece of timber, pointed and driven into the earth, as at the bottom of a river, or in a harbor where the ground is soft, for the support of a building or other superstructure. The stadthouse in Amsterdam is supported by piles.
pileate: Having the form of a cap or cover for the head.
pileated: Having the form of a cap or cover for the head.
piler: One who piles or forms a heap.
pileworm: A worm found in piles in Holland.
pilewort: A plant of the genus Ranunculus.
pilfer: To steal in small quantities; to practice petty theft; as a boy accustomed to pilfer. A pilfering hand.
To steal or gain by petty theft; to filch. He would not pilfer the victory, and the defeat was easy.
pilferer: One that pilfers or practices petty theft.
pilfering: Petty theft. Pilfering was so universal in all the South sea islands, that it was hardly recognized in the moral code of the natives as an offense, much less a crime.
pil-garlick: One who has lost his hair by disease; a poor forsaken wretch.
pilled-garlick: One who has lost his hair by disease; a poor forsaken wretch.
pilgrim: A wanderer; a traveler; particularly, one that travels to a distance from his own country to visit a holy place, or to pay his devotion to the remains of dead saints.
In Scripture, one that has only a temporary residence on earth. Hebrews 11:13.
pilgrimage: A long journey, particularly a journey to some place deemed sacred and venerable, in order to pay devotion to the relics of some deceased saint. Thus in the middle ages, kings, princes, bishops and others made pilgrimages to Jerusalem, in pious devotion to the Savior. Pilgrims now resort to Loretto, in Italy, to visit the chamber of the blessed virgin, and the Mohammedans make pilgrimages to Mecca, where their prophet was buried.
In Scripture, the journey of human life. Genesis 47:9.
pilgrimize: To wander about as a pilgrim.
pill: In pharmacy, a medicine in the form of a little ball or small round mass, to be swallowed whole.
To rob; to plunder; to pillage, that is, to peel, to strip.
To be peeled; to come off in flakes.
pillage: Plunder; spoil; that which is taken from another by open force, particularly and chiefly from enemies in war.
In architecture, a square pillar behind a column to bear up the arches.

To strip of money or goods by open violence; as, troops pillage the camp or towns of an enemy; to plunder; to spoil. It differs from stealing, as it implies open violence, and from robbery, which may be committed by one individual on another; whereas pillaging is usually the act of bands or numbers. To pillage and to rob are however sometimes used synonymously.
pillager: One that plunders by open violence; a plunderer.
pillar: Literally, a pile or heap; hence,

A kind or irregular column round an insulate, but deviating from the proportions of a just column. Pillars are either too massive or too slender for regular architecture; they are not restricted to any rules, and their parts and proportions are arbitrary. A square pillar is a massive work, called also a pier or piedroit, serving to support arches. etc.
A supporter; that which sustains or upholds; that on which some superstructure rests. Galatians 2:9.
A monument raised to commemorate any person or remarkable transaction. And Jacob set a pillar on her grave. Genesis 35:20; 2 Samuel 18:18.
Something resembling a pillar; as a pillar of salt. Genesis 19:26. So a pillar of a cloud, a pillar of fire. Exodus 13:21-22.
pillared: Having the form of a pillar.
pillion: A cushion for a woman to ride on behind a person on horseback.
A pad; a pannel; a low saddle.
The pad of a saddle that rests on the horse’s back.
pillory: A frame of wood erected on posts, with movable boards and holes, through which are put the head and hands of a criminal for punishment.
pillow: Along cushion to support the head of a person when reposing on a bed; a sack or case filled with feathers, down or other soft material.
In a ship, the block on which the inner end of a bowsprit is supported. The pillow of a plow, is a cross piece of wood which serves to raise or lower the beam.
To rest or lay on for support.
pillow-bier: The case or sack of a pillow which contains the feathers. Pillow-bier is the pillow-bearer.
pillow-case: The case or sack of a pillow which contains the feathers. Pillow-bier is the pillow-bearer.
pillowed: or a. Supported by a pillow.
pillowing: Resting or laying on a pillow.
pilose: Hairy. A pilose leaf, in botany, is one covered with long distinct hairs. A pilose receptacle has hairs between the florets.
pilous: Hairy. A pilose leaf, in botany, is one covered with long distinct hairs. A pilose receptacle has hairs between the florets.
pilot: One who steers a ship in a dangerous navigation, or rather one whose office or occupation is to steer ships, particularly along a coast, or into and out of a harbor, bay or river, where navigation is dangerous.
A guide; a director of the course of another person.
To direct the course of a ship in any place where navigation is dangerous.
pilotage: The compensation made or allowed to one who directs the course of a ship.
The pilot’s skill or knowledge of coasts, rocks, bars and channels.
pilot-fish: A fish, a species of Gasterosteus, called also rudder-fish, of an oblong shape; so named because it often accompanies ships.
piloting: Steering; as a ship in dangerous navigation.
The act of steering a ship.
pilser: The moth or fly that runs into a flame.
pimelite: A terrene substance of an apple green color, fat and unctuous to the touch, tender and not fusible by the blowpipe. It is supposed to be colored by nickel. It is a variety of steatite.
piment: Wine with a mixture of spice or honey.
pimento: Jamaica pepper, popularly called allspice. The tree producing this spice is of the genus Myrtus, and grows spontaneously in Jamaica in great abundance.
pimp: A man who provides gratifications for the lust of others; a procurer; a pander.
To pander; to procure lewd women for the gratification of others.
pimpernel: The name of several plants of different genera. The scarlet pimpernel is of the genus Anagallis, the water pimpernel of the genus Veronica, and the yellow pimpernel of the genus Lysimachia.
pimpinel: The name of several plants of different genera. The scarlet pimpernel is of the genus Anagallis, the water pimpernel of the genus Veronica, and the yellow pimpernel of the genus Lysimachia.
pimpillo: A plant of the genus Cactus.
pimpinella: A genus of plants, including the burnet saxifrage and the anise.
pimping: Pandering; procuring lewd women for others.
pimple: A small pustule on the face or other part of the body, usually a red pustule.
pimpled: Having red pustules on the skin; full of pimples.
pimplike: Like a pimp; vile; infamous; mean.
pin: A small pointed instrument made of brass wire and headed; used chiefly by females for fastening their clothes.

A piece of wood or metal sharpened or pointed, used to fasten together boards, plank or other timber. The larger pins of metal are usually called bolts, and the wooden pins used in ship building are called treenails I A small wooden pin is called a peg.
A thing of little value. It is not a pin’s matter. I care not a pin.
pincers: an erroneous orthography of pinchers, which see.
pinch: To press hard or squeeze between the ends of the fingers, the teeth, claws, or with an instrument.
To squeeze or compress between any two hard bodies.
To squeeze the flesh till it is pained or livid.
To gripe; to straiten; to oppress with want; as, to pinch a nation; to pinch the belly; to be pinched for want of food.
To pain by constriction; to distress; as pinching cold. The winter pinches.
pinchbeck: An alloy of copper; a mixture of copper and zink, consisting of three or four parts of copper with one of zink.
pinchers: An instrument for drawing nails from boards and the like, or for griping things to be held fast.
pincushion: A small case stuffed with some soft material, in which females stick pins for safety and preservation.
pindaric: After the style and manner of Pindar.
An ode in imitation of the odes of Pindar the Grecian, and prince of the lyric poets; an irregular ode.
pindust: Small particles of metal made by pointing pins.
pine: A tree of the genus Pinus, of many species, some of which furnish timber of the most valuable kind. The species which usually bear this name in the United States, are the white pine, Pinus strobus, the prince of our forests; the yellow pine, Pinus resinosa; and the pitch pine, Pinus rigida. The other species of this genus are called by other names, a fir, hemlock, larch, spruce

To languish; to lose flesh or wear away under any distress of anxiety of mind; to grow lean; followed sometimes by away. Ye shall not mourn nor weep, but ye shall pine away for your iniquities. Ezekiel 24:23.
To languish with desire; to waste away with longing for something; usually followed by for. Unknowing that she pin’d for your return.
To wear out; to make to languish. Where shivering cold and sickness pines the clime.& Beroe pined with pain.
To grieve for; to bemoan in silence. Abashed the devil stood-- . Virtue in her own shape how lovely, saw, And pined his loss.
pineal: The pineal gland is a part of the brain, about the bigness of a pea, situated in the third ventricle; so called from its shape. It was considered by Descartes as the seat of the soul.
pine-apple: The ananas, a species of Bromelia, so called from its resemblance to the cone of the pine tree.
pinery: A place where pine-apples are raised.
pin-fethered: Having the fethers only beginning to shoot; not fully fledged.
pinfold: A place in which beasts are confined. We now call it a pound.
pinhole: A small hole made by the puncture or perforation of a pin; a very small aperture.
pinion: The joint of a fowls wing, remotest from the body.
Awing. Hope humbly then, on trembling pinions soar.
The tooth of a smaller wheel, answering to that of a larger.
Fetters or bands for the arms.
pinirolo: A bird resembling the sandpiper, but larger; found in Italy.
pinite: A mineral holding a middle place between steatite and mica; the micarel of Kirwan. It is found in prismatic crystals of a greenish white color, brown or deep red. It occurs also massive.
pink: An eye, or a small eye; but now disused except in composition, as in pink-eyed, pink-eye.
A plant and flower of the genus Dianthus, common in our gardens.
A color used by painters; from the color of the flower.
A ship with a very narrow stern.
pin-sterned: Having a very narrow stern; as a ship.
pin-maker: One whose occupation is to make pins.
pin-money: A sum of money allowed or settled on a wife for her private expenses.
pinnace: A small vessel navigated with oars and sails, and having generally two masts rigged like those of a schooner; also, a boat usually rowed with eight oars.
pinnacle: A turret, or part of a building elevated above the main building. Some metropolis . With glistering spires and pinnacles adorn’d.
To build or furnish with pinnacles.
pinnate: In botany, a pinnate leaf is a species of compound leaf wherein a simple petiole has several leaflets attached to each side of it.
pinnated: In botany, a pinnate leaf is a species of compound leaf wherein a simple petiole has several leaflets attached to each side of it.
pinnatifid: In botany, fether-cleft. A pinnatifid leaf is a species of simple leaf, divided transversely by oblong horizontal segments or jags, not extending to the mid rib.
pinnatiped: Fin-footed; having the toes bordered by membranes.
pinner: One that pins or fastens; also, a pounder of cattle, or the pound keeper.
The lappet of a head which flies loose.
pinnite: Fossil remains of the Pinna, a genus of shells.
pinnulate: A pinnulate leaf is one in which each pinna is subdivided.
pint: Half a quart, or four gills. In medicine, twelve ounces. It is applied both to liquid and dry measure.
pintle: A little pin. In artillery, a long iron bolt.
pinules: In astronomy, the sights of an astrolabe.
pioneer: In the art and practice of war, one whose business is to march with or before an army, to repair the road or clear it of obstructions, work at intrenchments, or form mines for destroying an enemy’s works.
One that goes before to remove obstructions or prepare the way for another.
piony: An herbaceous perennial plant of the genus Paeonia, with tuberous roots, and bearing large beautiful red flowers. PION Y.
pious: Godly; reverencing and honoring the Supreme Being in heart and in the practice of the duties he has enjoined; having due veneration and affection for the character of God, and habitually obeying his commands; religious; devoted to the service of God; applied to persons.
Dictated by reverence to God; proceeding from piety; applied to things; as pious awe; pious services of affections; pious sorrow.N .
Having due respect and affection for parents or other relatives; practicing the duties of respect and affection towards parents or other near relatives.
Practiced under the pretense of religion; as pious frauds.
piously: In a pious manner; with reverence and affection for God; religiously; with due regard to sacred things or to the duties God has enjoined.
With due regard to natural or civil relations and to the duties which spring from them.
pip: A disease of fowls; a horny pellicle that grows on the tip of their tongue.
To cry or chirp, as a chicken; commonly pronounced peep.
pipe: To play on a wind instrument. 1 Corinthians 14:7.

A wind instrument of music, consisting of a long tube of wood or metal; as a rural pipe. The word, I believe, is not now the proper technical name of any particular instrument, but is applicable to any tubular wind instrument, and it occurs in bagpipe.
A long tube or hollow body; applied to the veins and arteries of the body, and to many hollow bodies, particularly such as are used for conductors of water or other fluids.
A tube of clay with a bowl at one end; used in smoking tobacco.
The organs of voice and respiration; as in windpipe.
pipe-fish: A fish of the genus Syngnathus.
piper: One who plays on a pipe or wind instrument.
piperidge: A shrub, the berberis, or barberry. The piperidge of New England is the nyssa villosa, a large tree with very tough wood.
piperin: A peculiar crystalline substance extracted from black pepper. The crystals of piperin are transparent, of a straw color, and they assume the tetrahedral prismatic form with oblique summits.
piping: Very hot; boiling; from the sound of boiling fluids.
pipistrel: A species of bat, the smallest of the kind.
pippin: A kind of apple; a tart apple. This name in America is given to several kinds of apples, as to the Newtown pippin, an excellent winter apple, and the summer pippin, a large apple, but more perishable than the Newtown pippin.
piquant: Pricking; stimulating to the tongue; as rock as piguant to the tongue as salt.
Sharp; tart; pungent; severe; as piquant railleries.
piquantly: pik’antly. With sharpness or pungency; tartly.
pique: An offense taken; usually, slight anger, irritation or displeasure at persons, rather temporary than permanent, and distinguished either in degree or temporariness from settled enmity or malevolence. Out of personal pique to those in service, he stands as a looker on, when the government is attacked.
Point; nicety; punctilio. Add long prescription of established laws, And pique of honor to maintain a cause.
To offend; to nettle; to irritate; to sting; to fret; to excite a degree of anger. It expresses less than exasperate. The lady was piqued by her indifference.
piquet: piket’. A game at cards played between two persons, with only thirty two cards; all the deuces, threes, fours, fives and sixes being set aside.
piracy: The act, practice or crime of robbing on the high seas; the taking of property from others by open violence and without authority, on the sea; a crime that answers to robbery on land. Other acts than robbery on the high seas, are declared by statute to be piracy. See Act of Congress, April 30, 1790.
The robbing of another by taking his writings.
pirate: A robber on the high seas; one that by open violence takes the property of another on the high seas. In strictness, the word pirate is one who makes it his business to cruise for robbery or plunder; a freebooter on the seas.
An armed ship or vessel which sails without legal commission, for the purpose of plundering other vessels indiscriminately on the high seas.
A bookseller that seizes the copies or writings of other men without permission.
To rob on the high seas.
To take by theft or without right or permission, as books or writings. They advertised they would pirate his edition.
pirated: Taken by theft or without right.
pirating: Robbing on the high seas; taking without right, as a book or writing. 1 .a. Undertaken for the sake of piracy; as a pirating expedition.
piratical: Robbing or plundering by open violence on the high seas; as a piratical commander or ship.
Consisting in piracy; predatory; robbing; as a piratical trade or occupation.
Practicing literary theft. The errors of the press were multiplied by piratical printers.
piragua: A canoe formed out of the truck of a tree, or two canoes united.
In modern usage in America, a narrow ferry boat carrying two masts and a leeboard.
pirry: A rough gale of wind; a storm.
piscary: In law, the right or privilege of fishing in another man’s waters.
piscation: The act or practice of fishing.
piscatory: Relating to fishes or to fishing; as a piscatory eclogue.
pisces: In astronomy, the Fishes, the twelfth sign or constellation in the zodiac.
piscine: Pertaining to fish or fishes; as piscine remains.
piscivorous: Feeding or subsisting on fishes. Many species of aquatic fowls are piscivorous.
pish: A word expressing contempt; sometimes spoken and written pshaw.
pisiform: Having the form of a pea. Masses of pisiform argillaceous iron ore.
pismire: The insect called the ant or emmet.
pisolite: Peastone, a carbonate of lime, slightly colored by the oxyd of iron. It occurs in little globular concretions of the size of a pea or larger, which usually contain each a grain of sand as a nucleus. These concretions in union sometimes compose entire beds of secondary mountains. It is sometimes called calcarious tufa.
pisophalt: Pea-mineral or mineral-pea; a soft bitumen, black and of a strong pungent smell. It appears to be petrol passing to asphalt. It holds a middle place between petrol, which is liquid, and asphalt, which is dry and brittle.
piss: To discharge the liquor secreted by the kidneys and lodged in the urinary bladder.
Urine; the liquor secreted by the kidneys into the bladder of an animal and discharged through the proper channel.
pissabed: The vulgar name of a yellow flower, growing among grass.
pissasphalt: Earth-pitch; pitch mixed with bitumen, natural or artificial; a fluid opake mineral substance, thick and inflammable, but leaving a residuum after burning.
pist: The track or foot-print of a horseman on the ground he goes over.
piste: The track or foot-print of a horseman on the ground he goes over.
pistachio: The nut of the Pistacia terebinthus or turpentine tree, containing a kernel of a pale greenish color, of a pleasant taste, resembling that of the almond, and yielding a well tasted oil. It is wholesome and nutritive. The tree grows in Syria, Arabia and Persia.
pistareen: A silver coin of the value of 17 or 18 cents, or 9d sterling.
pistil: In botany, the pointal, an organ of female flowers adhering to the fruit for the reception of the pollen, supposed to be a continuation of the pith, and when perfect, consisting of three parts, the germ or ovary, the style, and the stigma.
pistillaceous: Growing on the germ or seed bud of a flower.
pistillate: Having or consisting in a pistil.
pistillation: The act of pounding in a mortar.
pistilliferous: Having a pistil without stamens; as a female flower.
pistol: A small fire-arm, or the smallest fire-arm used, differing from a musket chiefly in size. Pistols are of different lengths, and borne by horsemen in cases at the saddle bow, or by a girdle. Small pistols are carried in the pocket.
pistole: A gold coin of Spain, but current in the neighboring countries.
piston: A short cylinder of metal or other solid substance, used in pumps and other engines or machines for various purposes. It is fitted exactly to the bore of another body so as to prevent the entrance or escape of air, and is usually applied to the purpose of forcing some fluid into or out of the canal or tube which it fills, as in pumps, fire- engines and the like.
pit: An artificial cavity made in the earth by digging; a deep hole in the earth.
A deep place; an abyss; profundity. Into what pit thou seest . From what height fallen.
The area for cock-fighting; whence the phrase, to fly the pit.
The middle part of a theater.
pitahaya: A shrub of California, which yields a delicious fruit, the Cactus Pitajaya.
pitapat: In a flutter; with palpitation or quick succession of beats; as, his heart went pitapat.
A light quick step. Now I hear the pitapat of a pretty foot, through the dark alley.
pitch: A thick tenacious substance, the juice of a species of pine or fir called abies picea, obtained by incision from the bark of the tree. When melted and pressed in bags of cloth, it is received into barrels. This is white or Burgundy pitch; by mixture with lampblack it is converted into black pitch. When kept long in fusion with vinegar, it becomes dry and brown, and forms colophony. The smoke of pitch condensed forms lampblack.
The resin of pine, or turpentine, inspissated; used in caulking ships and paying the sides and bottom.

Literally, a point; hence, any point or degree of elevation; as a high pitch; lowest pitch. How high a pitch his resolution soars. Alcibiades was one of the best orators of his age, notwithstanding he lived when learning was at its highest pitch.
Size; stature. So like in person, garb and pitch.
pitched: Set; planted; fixed; thrown headlong; set in array; smeared with pitch.
pitcher: An earthen vessel with a spout for pouring out liquors. This is its present signification. It seems formerly to have signified a water pot, jug or jar with ears.
An instrument for piercing the ground.
pitchfarthing: A play in which copper coin is pitched into a hole; called also chuck- farthing, from the root of choke.
pitchfork: A fork or farming utensil used in throwing hay or sheaves of grain, in loading or unloading carts and wagons.
pitching: Setting; planting or fixing; throwing headlong; plunging; daubing with pitch; setting, as a tune.
a. Declivous; descending; sloping; as a hill.
In navigation, the rising and falling of the head and stern of a ship, as she moves over waves; or the vertical vibration of a ship about her center of gravity.
pitchpipe: An instrument used by choristers in regulating the pitch or elevation of the key or leading note of a tune.
pitch-stone: A mineral, a sub-species of quartz, which in luster and texture resembles pitch, whence its name. It is sometimes called resinite. Its colors are, several shades of green; black with green, brown or gray; brown, tinged with red, green or yellow; sometimes yellowish or blue. It occurs in large beds and sometimes forms whole mountains.
pitchy: Partaking of the qualities of pitch; like pitch.
Black; dark; dismal; as the pitchy mantle of night.
pit-coal: Fossil coal; coal dug from the earth.
piteous: Sorrowful; mournful; that may excite pity; as a piteous look.
Wretched; miserable; deserving compassion; as a piteous condition.
Pitiful; paltry; poor; as piteous amends.
piteously: In a piteous manner; with compassion.
pitfall: A pit slightly covered for concealment, and intended to catch wild beasts or men.
pit-fish: A small fish of the Indian seas, about the size of a smelt, of a green and yellow color. It has the power of protruding or retracting its eyes at pleasure.
pith: The soft spungy substance in the center of plants and trees.
Energy; cogency; concentrated force; closeness and vigor of thought and style.
Condensed substance or matter; quintessence. The summary contains the pith of the original.
pithily: With strength; with close or concentrated force; cogently; with energy.
pithiness: Strength; concentrated force; as the pithiness of a reply.
pithy: Consisting of pith; containing pith; abounding with pith; as a pithy substance; a pithy stem.
Containing concentrated force; forcible; energetic; as a pithy word or expression. This pithy speech prevailed and all agreed.
Uttering energetic words or expressions. PITH Y. In all these, Goodman Fact was very short, but pithy.
pitiable: Deserving pity; worthy of compassion; miserable; as pitiable persons; a pitiable condition.
pitiful: Full of pity; tender; compassionate; having a heart to feel sorrow and sympathy for the distressed. James 5:11; 1 Peter 3:8.
Miserable; moving compassion; as a sight most pitiful; a pitiful condition.
To be pitied for its littleness or meanness; paltry; contemptible; despicable. That’s villainous, and shows a most pitiful ambition in the fool that uses it.
pitifully: With pity; compassionately. Pitifully behold the sorrows of our hearts.
In a manner to excite pity . They would sign and groan as pitifully as other men.
pitifulness: Tenderness of heart that disposes to pity; mercy; compassion.
pitiless: Destitute of pity; hardhearted; applied to persons; as a pitiless master.
Exciting no pity; as a pitiless state.
pitilessness: Unmercifulness; insensibility to the distresses of others.
pitman: The man that stands in a pit when sawing timber with another man who stands above.
pit-saw: A large saw used in dividing timber, and used by two men, one of whom stands in a pit below.
pittance: An allowance of meat in a monastery.
Avery small portion allowed or assigned.
pituitary: That secretes phlegm or mucus; as the pituitary membrane. The pituitary gland is a small oval body on the lower side of the brain, supposed by the ancients to secrete the mucus of the nostrils.
pituitous: Consisting of mucus, or resembling it in qualities.
pity: The feeling or suffering of one person, excited by the distresses of another; sympathy with the grief or misery of another; compassion or fellow-suffering. He that hath pity upon the poor lendeth to the Lord. Proverbs 19:17. In Scripture however, the word pity usually includes compassion accompanied with some act of charity or benevolence, and not simply a fellow feeling of distress. PIT Y. Pity is always painful, yet always agreeable.

The ground or subject of pity; cause of grief; thing to be regretted. What pity is it . That we can die but once to serve our country! That he is old, the more is the pity, his white hairs do witness it. In this sense, the word has a plural. It is a thousand pities he should waste his estate in prodigality.

é. To feel pain or grief for one in distress; to have sympathy for; to compassionate; to have tender feelings for one, excited by his unhappiness. Like as a father pitieth his children, so the Lord pitieth them that fear him. Psalm 103:13. Taught by that power who pities me, I learn to pity them.
To be compassionate; to exercise pity. I will not pity nor spare, nor have mercy. Jeremiah 13:14.
pivot: A pin on which any thing turns.
pix: A little box or chest in which the consecrated host is kept in Roman catholic countries.
A box used for the trial of gold and silver coin.
pizzle: In certain quadrupeds, the part which is official to generation and the discharge of urine.
placability: The quality of being appeasable; susceptibility of being pacified.
placableness: The quality of being appeasable; susceptibility of being pacified.
placable: That may be appeased or pacified; appeasable; admitting its passions or irritations to be allayed; willing to forgive. Methought I saw him placable and mild.
placard: Properly, a written or printed paper posted in a public place. It seems to have been formerly the name of an edict, proclamation or manifesto issued by authority, but this sense is, I believe, seldom or never annexed to the word. A placard now is an advertisement, or a libel, or a paper intended to censure public or private characters or public measures, posted in a public place. In the case of libels or papers intended to censure public or private characters, or the measures of government, these papers are usually pasted up at night for secrecy.
placate: To appease or pacify; to conciliate.
place: A particular portion of space of indefinite extent, occupied or intended to be occupied by any person or thing, and considered as the space where a person or thing does or may rest or has rested, as distinct from space in general. Look from the place where thou art. Genesis 13:14. The place where thou standest is holy ground. Exodus 3:5. Every place whereon the soles of your feet shall tread shall be yours. Deuteronomy 11:24. David’s place was empty. 7 Samuel 20:25, 27.
Any portion of space, as distinct from space in general. Enlargement and deliverance shall arise to the Jews from another place. Esther 4:14.
Local existence. From whose face the earth and the heaven fled away, and there was found no place for them. Revelation 20:11.
Separate room or apartment. His catalogue had an especial place for sequestered divines.
Seat; residence; mansion. The Romans shall come and take away both our place and nation. John 11:48.
place-man: One that has an office under a government.
placenta: In anatomy, the substance that connects the fetus to the womb, a soft roundish mass or cake by which the circulation is carried on between the parent and the fetus.
The part of a plant or fruit to which the seeds are attached.
placentation: In botany, the disposition of the cotyledons or lobes in the vegetation or germination of seeds.
placer: One who places, locates or sets.
placid: Gentle; quiet; undisturbed; equable; as a placid motion of the spirits.
Serene; mild; unruffled; indicating peace of mind; as a placid countenance or smile.
Calm; tranquil; serene; not stormy; as a placid sky.
Calm; quiet; unruffled; as a placid stream.
placidly: Mildly; calmly; quietly; without disturbance or passion.
placket: A petticoat. If this is the sense of the word in Shakespeare, it is a derivative. The word signifies the opening of the garment; but it is nearly or wholly obsolete.
plagiarism: The act of purloining another man’s literary works, or introducing passages from another man’s writings and putting them off as one’s own; literary theft.
plagiarist: One that purloins the writings of another and puts them off as his own.
plagiary: A thief in literature; one that purloins another’s writings and offers them to the public as his own.
plague: Any thing troublesome or vexatious; but in this sense, applied to the vexations we suffer from men, and not to the unavoidable evils inflicted on us by Divine Providence. The application of the word to the latter, would now be irreverent and reproachful.
A pestilential disease; an acute, malignant and contagious disease that often prevails in Egypt, Syria and Turkey, and has at times infected the large cities of Europe with frightful mortality.
Any great natural evil or calamity; as the ten plagues of Egypt.
plagueful: Abounding with plagues; infected with plagues.
plaguily: Vexatiously; in a manner to vex, harass or embarrass; greatly; horribly.
plaice: A fish of the genus Pleuronectes, growing to the size of eight or ten pounds or more. This fish is more flat and square than the halibut.
plaise: A fish of the genus Pleuronectes, growing to the size of eight or ten pounds or more. This fish is more flat and square than the halibut.
plaid: A striped or variegated cloth worn by the highlanders in Scotland. It is a narrow woolen stuff worn round the waist or on the shoulders, reaching to the knees, and in cold weather to the feet. It is worn by both sexes.
plad: A striped or variegated cloth worn by the highlanders in Scotland. It is a narrow woolen stuff worn round the waist or on the shoulders, reaching to the knees, and in cold weather to the feet. It is worn by both sexes.
plain: Smooth; even; level; flat; without elevations and depressions; not rough; as plain ground or land; a plain surface. In this sense, in philosophical writings, it is written plane.
Open; clear. Our troops beat an army in plain fight and open field.
Void of ornament; simple; as a plain dress. Plain without pomp, and rich without a show.
Artless; simple; unlearned; without disguise, cunning or affectation; without refinement; as men of the plainer sort. Genesis 25:27. Plain but pious christians-- .
Artless; simple; unaffected; unembellished; as a plain tale or narration.
plain-dealing: Dealing or communicating with frankness and sincerity; honest; open; speaking and acting without art; as a plain-dealing man.
A speaking or communicating with openness and sincerity; management without art, stratagem or disguise; sincerity.
plain-hearted: Having a sincere heart; communicating without art, reserve or hypocrisy; of a frank disposition.
plainly: Without ornament or artificial embellishment; as, to be plainly clad.
Frankly; honestly; sincerely; as, deal plainly with me.
plainness: Want of ornament; want of artificial show. So modest plainness sets off sprightly wit.
Openness; rough, blunt or unrefined frankness. Your plainness and your shortness please me well.
Artlessness; simplicity; candor; as unthinking plainness.
Clearness; openness; sincerity. Seeing then we have such hope, we use great plainness of speech. 2 Corinthians 3:12.
plain-song: The plain unvaried chant of churches; so called in contradistinction from the prick-song, or variegated music sung by note.
plaint: Lamentation; complaint; audible expression of sorrow. From inward grief . His bursting passion into plaints thus pour’d.
Complaint; representation made of injury or wrong done. There are three just grounds of war with Spain; one of plaints; two upon defense.
In law, a private memorial tendered to a court, in which the person sets forth his cause of action.
In law, a complaint; a formal accusation exhibited by a private person against an offender for a breach of law or a public offense.
plaintful: Complaining; expressing sorrow with an audible voice; as my plaintful tongue.
plaintif: In law, the person who commences a suit before a tribunal, for the recovery of a claim; opposed to defendant.
plaintive: Lamenting; complaining; expressive of sorrow; as a plaintive sound or song.
Complaining; expressing sorrow or grief; repining. To sooth the sorrows of her plaintive son.
plaintively: adv. In a manner expressive of grief.
plaintiveness: The quality or state of expressing grief.
plain-work: Plain needlework, as distinguished from embroidery.
plait: To fold; to double in narrow streaks; as, to plait a gown or a sleeve.
A fold; a doubling; as of cloth. It is very difficult to trace out the figure of a vest through all the plaits and folding of the drapery.
A braid of hair; a tress.
To braid; to interweave strands; as, to plait the hair.
plan: A draught or form; properly, the representation of any thing drawn on a plane, as a map or chart, which is a representation of some portion of land or water. But the word is applied particularly to the model of a building, showing the form, extent and divisions in miniature, and it may be applied to the draught or representation of any projected work on paper or on a plain surface; as the plan of a town or city, or of a harbor or fort. The form of a machine in miniature, is called a model.

A scheme devised; a project; the form of something to be done existing in the mind, with the several parts adjusted in idea, expressed in words or committed to writing; as the plan of a constitution of government; the plan of a treaty; the plan of an expedition.
To form a draught or representation of any intended work.
To scheme; to devise; to form in design; as, to plan the conquest of a country; to plan a reduction of taxes or of the national debt.
planch: To plank; to cover with planks or boards.
planched: Covered or made of planks or boards.
plancher: A floor. We owe obedience to the law of reason, which teacheth mediocrity in meats and drinks. MEDIOCRITY.
planchet: A flat piece of metal or coin.
planching: The laying of floors in a building; also, a floor of boards or planks.
plane: In geometry, an even or level surface, like plain in popular language.

In astronomy, an imaginary surface supposed to pass through any of the curves described on the celestial sphere; as the plane of the ecliptic; the plane of a planet’s orbit; the plane of a great circle.

In joinery and cabinet work, an instrument consisting of a smooth piece of wood, with an aperture, through which passes obliquely a piece of edged steel or chisel, used in paring or smoothing boards or wood of any kind.
To make smooth; to pare off the inequalities of the surface of a board or other piece of wood by the use of a plane.
planed: Made smooth with a plane; leveled.
planet: A celestial body which revolves about the sun or other center, or a body revolving about another planet as its center. The planets which revolve about the sun as their center, are called primary planets; those which revolve about other planets as their center, and with them revolve about the sun, are called secondary planets, satellites or moons. The primary planets are named Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn and Herschell. Four smaller planets, denominated by some, asteroids, namely, Ceres, Pallas, Juno and Vesta, have recently been discovered between the orbits of Mars and Jupiter. Mars, Jupiter, Saturn and Herschell, being without the earth’s orbit, are sometimes called the superior planets; Venus and Mercury, being within the earth’s orbit, are called inferior planets. The planets are opake bodies which receive their light from the sun. They are so named from their motion
planetarium: An astronomical machine which, by the movement of its parts, represents the motions and orbits of the planets, agreeable to the Copernican system.
planetary: Pertaining to the planets; as planetary inhabitants; planetary motions.
Consisting of planets; as a planetary system.
Under the dominion or influence of a planet; as a planetary hour.
Produced by planets; as planetary plague or influence.
Having the nature of a planet; erratic or revolving. Planetary days, the days of the week as shared among the planets, each having its day, as we name the days of the week after the planets.
plane-tree: A tree of the genus Platanus. The oriental plane-tree is a native of Asia; it rises with a straight smooth branching stem to a great highth, with palmated leaves and long pendulous peduncles, sustaining several heads of small close sitting flowers. The seeds are downy, and collected into round, rough, hard balls. The occidental plane-tree, which grows to a great highth, is a native of N. America; it is called also button-wood.
planet-struck: Affected by the influence of planets; blasted.
planifolious: In botany, a planifolious flower is one made up of plain leaves, set together in circular rows round the center. [See Planipetalous.,I.
planimetric: Pertaining to the mensuration of plain surfaces.
planimetrical: Pertaining to the mensuration of plain surfaces.
planimetry: The mensuration of plain surfaces, or that part of geometry which regards lines and plain figures, without considering their highth or depth.
planipetalous: In botany, flat-leafed, as when the small flowers are hollow only at the bottom, but flat upwards, as in dandelion and succory.
planish: To make smooth or plain; to polish; used by manufacturers.
planisphere: A sphere projected on a plane, in which sense, maps in which are exhibited the meridians and other circles, are planispheres.
plank: A broad piece of sawed timber, differing from a board only in being thicker. In America, broad pieces of sawed timber which are not more than an inch or an inch and a quarter thick, are called boards; like pieces from an inch and a half to three or four inches thick, are called planks. Sometimes pieces more than four inches thick are called planks.
To cover or lay with planks; as, to plank a floor or a ship.
planner: One who plans or forms a plan; a projector.
plano-conical: Plain or level on one side and conical on the other.
plano-convex: Plain or flat on one side and convex on the other; as a plano-convex lens.
planohorizontal: Having a level horizontal surface or position.
plant: A vegetable; an organic body, destitute of sense and spontaneous motion, adhering to another body in such a manner as to draw from it its nourishment, and having the power of propagating itself by seeds; “whose seed is in itself.” Genesis 1:11-

This definition may not be perfectly correct, as it respects all plants, for some marine plants grow without being attached to any fixed body. The woody or dicotyledonous plants consist of three parts; the bark or exterior coat, which covers the wood; the wood which is hard and constitutes the principal part; and the pith or center of the stem. In monocotyledonous plants, the ligneous or fibrous parts, and the pithy or parenchymatous, are equally distributed through the whole internal substance; and in the lower plants, funguses, sea weedthe substance is altogether parenchymatous. By means of proper vessels, the nourishing juices are distributed to every part of the plant. In its most general sense, plant comprehends. all vegetables, trees, shrubs, herbs, grassesIn popular language, the word is generally applied to the smaller species of vegetables.
In Scripture, a child; a descendant; the inhabitant of a country. Psalm 144:12; Jeremiah 48:32.
The sole of the foot. Sea-plant, a plant that grows in the sea or in salt water; sea weed. Sensitive plant, a plant that shrinks on being touched, the mimosa.
plantage: An herb, or herbs in general.
plantain: A plant of the genus Plantago, of several species. The water plantain is of the genus Alisma.

A tree of the genus Musa, the most remarkable species of which are, the paradisiaca or plantain, and the sapietum or banana tree. The plantain rises with a soft stem fifteen or twenty feet high, and the fruit is a substitute for bread.
plantain-tree: A tree of the genus Musa, the most remarkable species of which are, the paradisiaca or plantain, and the sapietum or banana tree. The plantain rises with a soft stem fifteen or twenty feet high, and the fruit is a substitute for bread.
plantation: The act of planting or setting in the earth for growth.
The place planted; applied to ground planted with trees, as an orchard or the like.

In the United States and the West Indies, a cultivated estate; a farm. In the United States, this word is applied to an estate, a tract of land occupied and cultivated, in those states only where the labor is performed by slaves, and where the land is more or less appropriated to the culture of tobacco, rice, indigo and cotton, that is, from Maryland to Georgia inclusive, on the Atlantic, and in the western states where the land is appropriated to the same articles or to the culture of the sugar cane. From Maryland, northward and eastward, estates in land are called farms.
An original settlement in a new country; a town or village planted. While these plantations were forming in Connecticut-- .
plant-cane: In the West Indies, the original plants of the sugar cane, produced from germs placed in the ground; or canes of the first growth, in distinction from the ratoons, or sprouts from the roots of canes which have been cut.
planted: Set in the earth for propagation; set; fixed; introduced; established.
Furnished with seeds or plants for growth; as a planted field.
Furnished with the first inhabitants; settled; as territory planted with colonists.
Filled or furnished with what is new. A man in all the world’s new fashion planted.
planter: One that plants, sets, introduces or establishes; as a planter of maiz; a planter of vines; the planters of a colony.
One that settles in a new or uncultivated territory; as the first planters in Virginia!
One who owns a plantation; used in the West Indies and southern states of America.
One that introduces and establishes. The apostles were the first planters of christianity.
plantership: The business of a planter, or the management of a plantation, as in the West Indies.
planticle: A young plant or plant in embryo.
planting: Setting in the earth for propagation; setting; settling; introducing; establishing.
The act or operation of setting in the ground for propagation, as seeds, trees, shrubs
plant-louse: An insect that infests plants; a vine fretter; the puceron.
plash: Asmall collection of standing water; a puddle.
The branch of a tree partly cut or lopped and bound to other branches.
To dabble in water; usually splash.
To interweave branches; as, to plash a hedge or quicksets.
plashing: Cutting and interweaving, as branches in a hedge.
The act or operation of cutting and lopping small trees and interweaving them, as in hedges.
plasm: A mold or matrix in which any thing is cast or formed to a particular shape.
plasma: A silicious mineral of a color between grass green and leek green, occurring in angular pieces in beds, associated with common chalcedony, and among the ruins of Rome.
plasmatic: Giving shape; having the power of giving form.
plasmatical: Giving shape; having the power of giving form.
plaster: A composition of lime, water and sand, well mixed into a kind of paste and used for coating walls and partitions of houses. This composition when dry becomes hard, but still retains the name of plaster. Plaster is sometimes made of different materials, as chalk, gypsumand is sometimes used to parget the whole surface of a building.

In pharmacy, an external application of a harder consistence than an ointment, to be spread, according to different circumstances, either on linen or leather. Plaster of Paris, a composition of several species of gypsum dug near Montmartre, near Paris in France, used in building and in casting busts and statues. In popular language, this name is applied improperly to plaster-stone, or to any species of gypsum.
plasterer: One that makes figures in plaster.
plastering: Covering with or laying on plaster.
The act or operation of overlaying with plaster.
The plaster-work of a building; a covering of plaster.
plaster-stone: Gypsum, which see. This when pulverized is extensively used as a manure.
plastic: Having the power to give form or fashion to a mass of matter; as the plastic hand of the Creator; the plastic virtue of nature.
plasticity: The quality of giving form or shape to matter.
plastron: A piece of leather stuffed; used by fencers to defend the body against pushes.
plat: To weave; to form by texture. Matthew 27:29.
Work done by platting or interweaving.
A small piece of ground, usually a portion of flat even ground; as a flowery plat; a plat of willows.
platting: Work done by platting or interweaving.
platband: A border of flowers in a garden, along a wall or the side of a parterre.
In architecture, a flat square molding whose highth much exceeds its projecture, such as the faces of an architrave.
The lintel of a door or window.
A list or fillet between the flutings of a column.
plate: A piece of metal, flat or extended in breadth.
Armor of plate, composed of broad pieces, and thus distinguished from mail.
A piece of wrought silver, as a dish or other shallow vessel; hence, vessels of silver; wrought silver in general. Plate, by the laws of some states, is subject to a tax by the ounce.
A small shallow vessel, made of silver or other metal, or of earth glazed and baked, from which provisions are eaten at table. A wooden plate is called a trencher.
The prize given for the best horse in a race.
plated: Covered or adorned with plate; armed with plate; beaten into plates.
platen: Among printers, the flat part of a press by which the impression is made.
platform: The sketch of any thing horizontally delineated; the ichnography.
A place laid out after any model.
In the military art, an elevation of earth or a floor of wood or stone, on which cannons are mounted to fire on an enemy.
In architecture, a row of beams or a piece of timber which supports the timber-work of a roof, and lying on the top of the wall. This in New England is called the plate.
A kind of terrace or broad smooth open walk on the top of a building, as in the oriental houses.
platina: A metal discovered in the mines of Choco in Peru, nearly of the color of silver, but less bright, and the heaviest of the metals. Its specific gravity is to that of water as 23 to 1. It is harder than iron, undergoes no alteration in air, resists the action of acids and alkalies, is very ductile and capable of being rolled into thin plates.
platinum: A metal discovered in the mines of Choco in Peru, nearly of the color of silver, but less bright, and the heaviest of the metals. Its specific gravity is to that of water as 23 to 1. It is harder than iron, undergoes no alteration in air, resists the action of acids and alkalies, is very ductile and capable of being rolled into thin plates.
plating: Overlaying with plate or with a metal; beating into thin lamens.

The art or operation of covering any thing with plate or with a metal, particularly of overlaying a baser metal with a thin plate of silver. The coating of silver is soldered to the metal with tin or a mixture of three parts of silver with one of brass.
platonic: Pertaining to Plato the philosopher, or to his philosophy, his school or his opinions. Platonic love, is a pure spiritual affection subsisting between the sexes, unmixed with carnal desires, and regarding the mind only and its excellencies; a species of love for which Plato was a warm advocate. Platonic year, the great year, or a period of time determines by the revolution of the equinoxes, or the space of time in which the stars and constellations return to their former places in respect to the equinoxes. This revolution, which is calculated by the precession of the equinoxes, is accomplished in about 25,000 years.
platonism: The philosophy of Plato, consisting of three branches, theology, physics and mathematics. Under theology is included moral philosophy. The foundation of Plato’s theology is the opinion that there are two eternal, primary, independent and incorruptible principles or causes of all things, and matter, from which all things are made. It was a fundamental maxim with him that from nothing, nothing can proceed. While therefore he held God to be the maker of the universe, he held matter, the substance of which the universe was made, to be eternal.
platonist: One that adheres to the philosophy of Plato; a follower of Plato.
platonizer: One that adheres to the philosophy of Plato; a follower of Plato.
platonize: To adopt the opinions or philosophy of Plato.
To explain on the principles of the Platonic school, or to accommodate to those principles.
platonized: Accommodated to the philosophy of Plato.
platonizing: Adopting the principles of Plato; accommodating to the principles of the Platonic school.
platoon: A small square body of soldiers or musketeers, drawn out of a battalion of foot when they form a hollow square, to strengthen the angles; or a small body acting together, but separate from the main body; as, to fire by platoons.
platter: A large shallow dish for holding the provisions of a table.
One that plats or forms by weaving.
platypus: A quadruped of New Holland, whose jaws are elongated into the shape of a duck’s bill. The body is covered with thick hair and the feet are webbed. This animal has been arranged with the Mammalia, but it is now presumed to be oviparous; at least its breasts have not hitherto been observed.
plausibility: s as z. Speciousness; superficial appearance of right.
plausible: That may be applauded; that may gain favor or approbation; hence, superficially pleasing; apparently right; specious; popular; as a plausible argument; a plausible’ pretext; a plausible doctrine.
Using specious arguments or discourse; as a plausible man.
plausibleness: Speciousness; show of right or propriety; as the plausibleness of Arminianism.
plausibly: With fair show; speciously; in a matter adapted to gain favor or approbation. They could talk plausibly about what they’ did not understand.
play: To use any exercise for pleasure or recreation; to do something not as a task or for profit, but for amusement; as, to play at cricket. The people sat down to eat and to drink, and rose up to play. Exodus 32:6.
To sport; to frolick; to frisk. The lamb thy riot dooms to bleed to day, Had he thy reason, would he skip and play?
To toy; to act with levity.
To trifle; to act wantonly and thoughtlessly. Men are apt to play with their healths and their lives as they do with their clothes.
To do something fanciful; to give a fanciful turn to; as, to play upon words.
playbill: A printed advertisement of a play, with the parts assigned to the actors.
play-day: A day given to play or diversion; a day exempt from work.
playing-day: A day given to play or diversion; a day exempt from work.
player: One who plays in any game or sport.
An actor of dramatic scenes; one whose occupation is to imitate characters on the stage.
One who performs on an instrument of music.
playfellow: A companion in amusements or sports.
playful: Sportive; given to levity; as a playful child.
Indulging a sportive fancy; as a playful genius.
playhouse: A house appropriated to the exhibition of dramatic compositions; a theater.
playmate: A playfellow; a companion in diversions.
plaything: A toy; any thing that serves to amuse. A child knows his nurse, and by degrees the playthings of a little more advanced age.
plea: In law, that which is alleged by a party in support of his demand; but in a more limited and technical sense, the answer of the defendant to the plaintiff's declaration and demand. That which the plaintiff alleges in his declaration is answered and repelled or justified by the defendant's plea. Pleas are dilatory, or pleas to the action. Dilatory pleas, are to the jurisdiction of the court, to the disability of the plaintiff, or in abatement. Pleas to the action are an answer to the merits of the complaint, which confesses or denies it. Pleas that deny the plaintiffs complaint or demand, are the general issue, which denies the whole declaration; or special pleas in bar, which state something which precludes the plaintiffs right of recovery.
A cause in court; a lawsuit, or a criminal process; as the pleas of the crown; the court of common pleas. The supreme judicial court shall have cognizance of pleas real, personal and mixed.
That which is alleged in defense or justification; an excuse; an apology; as the tyrant’s plea. When such occasions are, No plea must serve; ‘tis cruelty to spare.
plead: In a general sense, to argue in support of a claim, or in defense against the claim of another.

In law, to present an answer to the declaration of a plaintiff; to deny the plaintiffs declaration and demand, or to allege facts which show that he ought not to recover in the suit. The plaintiff declares or alleges; the defendant pleads to his declaration. The king or the state prosecutes an offender, and the offender pleads not guilty, or confesses the charge.

To urge reasons for or against; to attempt to persuade one by argument or supplication; as, to plead for the life of a criminal; to plead in his favor; to plead with a judge or with a father. O that one might plead for a man with God, as a man pleadeth for his neighbor! Job 16:21.
To urge; to press by operating on the passions. Since you can love, and yet your error see, The same resistless power may plead for me.
pleadable: That may be pleaded; that may be alleged in proof, defense or vindication; as a right or privilege pleadable at law.
pleaded: Offered or urged in defense; alleged in proof or support.
pleader: One who argues in a court of justice.
One that forms pleas or pleadings; as a special pleader.
One that offers reasons for or against; one that attempts to maintain by arguments. So fair a pleader any cause may gain.
pleading: Offering in defense; supporting by arguments or reasons; supplicating.
The art of supporting by arguments, or of reasoning to persuade.
pleadings: In law, the mutual altercations between the plaintiff and defendant, or written statements of the parties in support of their claims, comprehending the declaration, count or narration of the plaintiff, the plea of the defendant in reply, the replication of the plaintiff to the defendant’s plea, the defendant’s rejoinder, the plaintiff's sur-rejoinder, the defendant’s rebutter, the plaintiff's sur-rebuttertill the question is brought to issue, that is, to rest on a single point.
pleasant: Pleasing; agreeable; grateful to the mind or to the senses; as a pleasant ride; a pleasant voyage; a pleasant view. Light is pleasant to the eye; an orange is pleasant to the taste; harmony is pleasant to the ear; a rose is pleasant to the smell. How good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity! Psalm 133:1.
Cheerful; enlivening; as pleasant society or company.
Gay; lively; humorous; sportive; as a pleasant companion.
Trifling; adapted rather to mirth than use.
pleasantly: plez’antly. In such a manner as to please or gratify.
pleasantness: plez’antness. State of being pleasant or agreeable; as the pleasantness of a situation.
Cheerfulness; gayety; merriment; as the pleasantness of youth.
pleasantry: plez’antry. Gayety; merriment. The harshness of reasoning is not a little softened and smoothed by the infusions of mirth and pleasantry.
Sprightly saying; lively talk; effusion of humor. The grave abound in pleasantries, the dull in repartees and points of wit.
please: To excite agreeable sensations or emotions in; to gratify; as, to please the taste; to please the mind. Their words pleased Hamor, and Shechem, Hamor’s son. Genesis 34:18. Leave such to trifle with more grace than ease, Whom folly pleases, and whose follies please.
To Satisfy; to content. What next I bring shall please . Thy wish exactly to thy heart’s desire.

To prefer; to have satisfaction in; to like; to choose. Many of our most skilful painters were pleased or recommend this author to me. To be pleased in or with, to approve; to have complacency in. Matthew 3:17. To please God, is to love his character and law and perform his will, so as to become the object of his approbation. They that are in the flesh cannot please God. Romans 8:8.
s as z. To like; to choose; to prefer. Spirits, freed from mortal laws, with ease . Assume what sexes and what shapes they please.

To condescend; to comply; to be pleased; a word of ceremony. Please you, lords, In sight of both our battles we may meet. The first words that I learnt were, to express my desire that he would please to give me my liberty. Please expresses less gratification than delight.
pleased: Gratified; affected with agreeable sensations or emotions.
pleaseman: An officious person who courts favor servilely; a pickthank.
pleaser: One that pleases or gratifies; one that courts favor by humoring or flattering compliances or a show of obedience; as men-pleasers. Ephesians 6:6; Colossians 3:22.
pleasing: Gratifying; exciting agreeable sensations or emotions in.
Giving pleasure or satisfaction; agreeable to the senses or to the mind; as a pleasing prospect; a pleasing reflection; pleasing manners.
pleasingly: In such a manner as to give pleasure.
pleasurable: plezh’urable. Pleasing; giving pleasure; affording gratification. Planting of orchards is very profitable as well as pleasurable.
pleasurably: With pleasure; with gratification of the senses or the mind.
pleasure: The gratification of the senses or of the mind; agreeable sensations or emotions; the excitement, relish or happiness produced by enjoyment or the expectation of good; opposed to pain. We receive pleasure from the indulgence of appetite; from the view of a beautiful landscape; from the harmony of sounds; from agreeable society; from the expectation of seeing an absent friend; from the prospect of gain or success of any kind. Pleasure, bodily and mental, carnal and spiritual, constitutes the whole of positive happiness, as pain constitutes the whole of misery. Pleasure is properly positive excitement of the passions or the mind; but we give the name also to the absence of excitement, when that excitement is painful; as when we cease to labor, or repose after fatigue, or when the mind is tranquilized after anxiety or agitation. Pleasure is susceptible of increase to any degree; but the wor
Approbation. The Lord taketh pleasure in his people. Psalms 147:11; Psalms 149:4.

What the will dictates or prefers; will; choice; purpose; intention; command; as, use your pleasure. Cyrus, he is my shepherd and shall perform all my pleasure. Isaiah 44:28. My counsel shall stand and I will do all my pleasure. IsaIah 46:10.
A favor; that which pleases. Festus, willing to do the Jews a pleasure, answered Paul. Acts 25:9.
pleasure-boat: A boat appropriated to sailing for amusement.
pleasure-ground: Ground laid out in an ornamental manner and appropriated to pleasure or amusement.
pleasurist: A person devoted to worldly pleasure.
plebeian: Pertaining to the common people; vulgar; as plebeian minds; plebeian sports.
Consisting of common people; as a plebeian throng.
One of the common people or lower ranks of men.
pledge: Something put in pawn; that which is deposited with another as security for the repayment of money borrowed, or for the performance of some agreement or obligation; a pawn. A borrows ten pounds of B, and deposits his watch as a pledge that the money shall be repaid; and by repayment of the money, A redeems the pledge.

Any thing given or considered as a security for the performance of an act. Thus a man gives a word or makes a promise to another, which is received as a pledge for fulfillment. The mutual affection of husband and wife is a pledge for the faithful performance of the marriage covenant. Mutual interest is the best pledge for the performance of treaties.

In law, a gage or security real or personal, given for the repayment of money. It is of two kinds; vadium vivum, a living pledge, as when a man borrows money and grants an estate to be held by the pledgee, till the rents and profits shall refund the money, in which case the land or pledge is said to be living; or it is vadium mortuum, a dead pledge, called a mortgage.

In law, bail; surety given for the prosecution of a suit, or for the appearance of a defendant, or for restoring goods taken in distress and replevied. The distress itself is also called a pledge, and the glove formerly thrown down by a champion in trial by battel, was a pledge by which the champion stipulated to encounter his antagonist in that trial.
pledged: Deposited as security; given in warrant.
pledgee: The person to whom any thing is pledged.
pledger: One that pledges or pawns any thing; one that warrants or secures.
One that accepts the invitation to drink after another, or that secures another by drinking.
pledget: In surgery, a compress or small flat tent of lint, laid over a wound to imbibe the matter discharged and keep it clean.
pledging: Depositing in pawn or as security; giving warrant for security or safety.
pleiads: ple’yads. In astronomy, a cluster of seven stars in the neck of the constellation Taurus. The Latins called them Vergilioe, from ver, spring, because of their rising about the vernal equinox.
plenarty: The state of a benefice when occupied.
plenary: Full; entire; complete; as a plenary license; plenary consent; plenary indulgence. The plenary indulgence of the pope is an entire remission of penalties to all sins.
